25.anchor: He sent them more than enough to eat.
25.note: tn Heb “provision he sent to them to satisfaction.”

27.anchor: birds as numerous as the sand on the seashores.
27.note: tn Heb “and like the sand of the seas winged birds.”

29.anchor: They ate until they were stuffed;
29.note: tn Heb “and they ate and were very satisfied.”

30.anchor: They were not yet filled up,
30.note: tn Heb “they were not separated from their desire.”

32.anchor: and did not trust him to do amazing things.
32.note: tn Heb “and did not believe in his amazing deeds.”

33.note: tn Heb “and their years in terror.”

34.note: tn Or “killed them,” that is, killed large numbers of them.

35.anchor: and that the sovereign God was their deliverer.
35.note: tn Heb “and [that] God Most High [was] their redeemer.”

36.note: tn Heb “and with their tongue they lied to him.”

37.anchor: They were not really committed to him,
37.note: tn Heb “and their heart was not firm with him.”

38.anchor: and does not stir up his fury.
38.note: tn One could translate v. 38 in the past tense (“he was compassionate…forgave sin and did not destroy…held back his anger, and did not stir up his fury”), but the imperfect verbal forms are probably best understood as generalizing. Verse 38 steps back briefly from the narrational summary of Israel’s history and lays the theological basis for v. 39, which focuses on God’s mercy toward sinful Israel.

39.anchor: and were like a wind that blows past and does not return.
39.note: tn Heb “and he remembered that they [were] flesh, a wind [that] goes and does not return.”

41.note: tn Or “wounded, hurt.” The verb occurs only here in the OT.

sn The basic sense of the word “holy” is “set apart from that which is commonplace, special, unique.” The Lord’s holiness is first and foremost his transcendent sovereignty as the ruler of the world. He is “set apart” from the world over which he rules. At the same time his holiness encompasses his moral authority, which derives from his royal position. As king he has the right to dictate to his subjects how they are to live; indeed his very own character sets the standard for proper behavior. This expression is a common title for the Lord in the book of Isaiah.

42.anchor: how he delivered them from the enemy,
42.note: tn Heb “[the] day [in] which he ransomed them from [the] enemy.”

43.note: tn Or “portents, omens” (see Ps 71:7). The Egyptian plagues are referred to here (see vv. 44-51).

45.anchor: as well as frogs that overran their land.
45.note: tn Heb “and a swarm of frogs and it destroyed them.”

48.anchor: and hurled lightning bolts down on their livestock.
48.note: tn Heb “and their livestock to the flames.” “Flames” here refer to the lightning bolts that accompanied the storm.

49.note: tn Heb “fury and indignation and trouble, a sending of messengers of disaster.”

50.anchor: he handed their lives over to destruction.

51.anchor: the firstfruits of their reproductive power
51.note: tn Heb “the beginning of strength.” If retained, the plural form אוֹנִים (’onim, “strength”) probably indicates degree (“great strength”), but many ancient witnesses read “their strength,” which presupposes an emendation to אֹנָם (’onam; singular form of the noun with third masculine plural pronominal suffix).

54.note: tn Heb “this mountain.” The whole land of Canaan seems to be referred to here. In Exod 15:17 the promised land is called the “mountain of your [i.e., God’s] inheritance.”
tn The “right hand” here symbolizes God’s military strength (see v. 55).

55.anchor: and allowed the tribes of Israel to settle down.
55.note: tn Heb “and caused the tribes of Israel to settle down in their tents.”

57.anchor: they were as unreliable as a malfunctioning bow.
57.note: tn Heb “they turned aside like a deceitful bow.”

58.anchor: They made him angry with their pagan shrines,

61.anchor: he gave the symbol of his splendor
61.note: tn Heb “and his splendor into the hand of an enemy.” The expression “his splendor” also refers metonymically to the ark of the covenant.

61.anchor: into the hand of the enemy.
61.note: sn Verses 60-61 refer to the Philistines’ capture of the ark in the days of Eli (1 Sam 4:1-11).

62.anchor: and was angry with his chosen nation.

63.note: tn Heb “his.” The singular pronominal suffix is collective, referring back to God’s “people” (v. 62).
tn Heb “were not praised,” that is, in wedding songs. The young men died in masses, leaving no husbands for the young women.

64.note: tn Heb “his.” The singular pronominal suffix is collective, referring back to God’s “people” (v. 62).
sn Because of the invading army and the ensuing panic, the priests’ widows had no time to carry out the normal mourning rites.

65.anchor: he was like a warrior in a drunken rage.
65.note: tn Heb “like a warrior overcome with wine.” The Hebrew verb רוּן (run, “overcome”) occurs only here in the OT. The phrase “overcome with wine” could picture a drunken warrior controlled by his emotions and passions (as in the present translation), or it could refer to a warrior who awakes from a drunken stupor.

66.anchor: he made them a permanent target for insults.
66.note: tn Heb “a permanent reproach he made them.”

69.anchor: as secure as the earth, which he established permanently.
69.note: tn Heb “like the earth, [which] he established permanently.” The feminine singular suffix on the Hebrew verb יָסַד (yasad, “to establish”) refers to the grammatically feminine noun “earth.”

71.anchor: and of Israel, his chosen nation.
71.note: tn Heb “to shepherd Jacob, his people, and Israel, his inheritance.”

72.note: tn Heb “and with the understanding of his hands he led them.”

1.note: tn Traditionally, “Lord of hosts.” The title draws attention to God’s sovereign position (see Ps 69:6).

2.anchor: My heart and my entire being
2.note: tn Heb “my flesh,” which stands for his whole person and being.

3.anchor: where she can protect her young
3.note: tn Heb “even a bird finds a home, and a swallow a nest for herself, [in] which she places her young.”sn The psalmist here romanticizes the temple as a place of refuge and safety. As he thinks of the birds nesting near its roof, he envisions them finding protection in God’s presence.

4.note: tn The Hebrew noun is an abstract plural. The word often refers metonymically to the happiness that God-given security and prosperity produce (see v. 12 and Pss 1:1; 2:12; 34:9; 41:1; 65:4; 89:15; 106:3; 112:1; 127:5; 128:1; 144:15).

5.anchor: and long to travel the roads that lead to your temple!
5.note: tn Heb “roads [are] in their heart[s].” The roads are here those that lead to Zion (see v. 7).

6.note: tn This rare word may refer to the early (or autumn) rains (see Joel 2:23).

6.anchor: even covers it with pools of water.
6.note: tc The MT reads בְּרָכוֹת (bÿrakhot, “blessings”) but the preceding reference to a “spring” favors an emendation to בְּרֵכוֹת (bÿrekhot, “pools”).sn Pools of water. Because water is so necessary for life, it makes an apt symbol for divine favor and blessing. As the pilgrims traveled to Jerusalem, God provided for their physical needs and gave them a token of his favor and of the blessings awaiting them at the temple.

7.note: tn The psalmist returns to the singular (see v. 5a), which he uses in either a representative or distributive (“each one” ) sense.

8.note: tn Heb “Lord, God, hosts.” One expects the construct form אֱלֹהֵי before צְבָאוֹת (tsÿva’ot, “hosts”; see Ps 89:9) but יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים (yehvah ’elohim) precedes צְבָאוֹת in Pss 59:5 and 80:4, 19 as well.

9.anchor: Show concern for your chosen king!
9.note: tn Heb “look [on] the face of your anointed one.” The Hebrew phrase מְשִׁיחֶךָ (mÿshikhekha, “your anointed one”) refers here to the Davidic king (see Pss 2:2; 18:50; 20:6; 28:8; 89:38, 51; 132:10, 17).

10.note: tn The verb דּוּר (dur, “to live”) occurs only here in the OT.

11.anchor: he withholds no good thing from those who have integrity.
11.note: tn Heb “he does not withhold good to those walking in integrity.”

12.anchor: how blessed are those who trust in you!
12.note: tn Heb “[Oh] the happiness [of] the man [who] trusts in you.” Hebrew literature often assumes and reflects the male-oriented perspective of ancient Israelite society. The principle stated here is certainly applicable to all people, regardless of their gender or age. To facilitate modern application, we translate the gender and age specific “man” with the plural “those.” The individual referred to here is representative of all followers of God, as the use of the plural form in v. 12b indicates.

1.anchor: you restored the well-being of Jacob.
1.note: tn Heb “you turned with a turning [toward] Jacob.” The Hebrew term שְׁבוּת (shÿvut) is apparently a cognate accusative of שׁוּב (shuv). See Pss 14:7; 53:6.

2.note: tn Heb “covered over.”

3.anchor: you turned back from your raging anger.
3.note: tn Heb “the rage of your anger.” The phrase “rage of your anger” employs an appositional genitive. Synonyms are joined in a construct relationship to emphasize the single idea. For a detailed discussion of the grammatical point with numerous examples, see Y. Avishur, “Pairs of Synonymous Words in the Construct State (and in Appositional Hendiadys) in Biblical Hebrew,” Semitics 2 (1971): 17-81. See Pss 69:24; 78:49.

4.anchor: Do not be displeased with us!
4.note: tn Heb “break your displeasure with us.” Some prefer to emend הָפֵר (hafer, “break”) to הָסֵר (haser, “turn aside”).

5.anchor: Will you remain angry throughout future generations?
5.note: tn Heb “Will your anger stretch to a generation and a generation?”

8.note: tn Or “yet let them not.” After the negative particle אֵל (’el), the prefixed verbal form is jussive, indicating the speaker’s desire or wish.

9.anchor: then his splendor will again appear in our land.
9.note: tn Heb “to dwell, glory, in our land.” “Glory” is the subject of the infinitive. The infinitive with -לְ (lÿ), “to dwell,” probably indicates result here (“then”). When God delivers his people and renews his relationship with them, he will once more reveal his royal splendor in the land.

10.anchor: deliverance and peace greet each other with a kiss.
10.note: sn Deliverance and peace greet each other with a kiss. The psalmist personifies these abstract qualities to emphasize that God’s loyal love and faithfulness will yield deliverance and peace for his people.

11.anchor: and deliverance looks down from the sky.
11.note: sn The psalmist already sees undeniable signs of God’s faithfulness and expects deliverance to arrive soon.

12.anchor: and our land will yield
12.note: tn Both “bestow” and “yield” translate the same Hebrew verb (נָתַן, natan). The repetition of the word emphasizes that agricultural prosperity is the direct result of divine blessing.

13.note: tn Or “will prepare.”

tn Heb “and it prepares for a way his footsteps.” Some suggest emending וְיָשֵׂם (vÿyasem, “and prepares”) to וְשָׁלוֹם (vÿshalom, “and peace”) since “deliverance” and “peace” are closely related earlier in v. 13. This could be translated, “and peace [goes ahead, making] a pathway for his footsteps” (cf. NEB).

1.note: tn Heb “turn your ear.”

2.note: tn Heb “my life.”

3.note: tn Or “show me favor.”

4.anchor: for to you, O Lord, I pray!
4.note: tn Heb “I lift up my soul.”

5.anchor: Certainly
5.note: tn Or “for.”

5.anchor: O Lord, you are kind
5.note: tn Heb “good.”

8.note: tn Heb “and there are none like your acts.”

9.note: tn Or “bow down before you.”

11.anchor: Make me wholeheartedly committed to you!
11.note: tn Heb “Bind my heart to the fearing of your name.” The verb translated “bind” occurs only here in the Piel stem. It appears twice in the Qal, meaning “be joined” in both cases (Gen 49:6; Isa 14:20). To “fear” God’s name means to have a healthy respect for him which in turn motivates one to obey his commands (see Pss 61:5; 102:15).

12.anchor: I will honor your name continually!
12.note: tn Or “forever.”

13.note: tn Or “for he will have delivered my life.” The verb form indicates a future perfect here.
tn Or “lower Sheol.”

14.note: tn Or “assembly.”

14.anchor: of ruthless men, who do not respect you, seek my life.
14.note: tn Heb “seek my life and do not set you before them.” See Ps 54:3.

15.anchor: You are patient
15.note: tn Heb “slow to anger.”

15.anchor: and demonstrate great loyal love and faithfulness.
15.note: tn Heb “and great of loyal love and faithfulness.”sn The psalmist’s confession of faith in this verse echoes Exod 34:6.

16.anchor: Deliver your slave!
16.note: tn Heb “the son of your female servant.” The phrase “son of a female servant” (see also Ps 116:16) is used of a son born to a secondary wife or concubine (Exod 23:12). In some cases the child’s father is the master of the house (see Gen 21:10, 13; Judg 9:18). The use of the expression here certainly does not imply that the Lord has such a secondary wife or concubine! It is used metaphorically and idiomatically to emphasize the psalmist’s humility before the Lord and his status as the Lord’s servant.

17.anchor: , will help me and comfort me.
17.note: tn The perfect verbal forms are understood here as dramatic/rhetorical, expressing the psalmist’s certitude that such a sign from the Lord will be followed by his intervention. Another option is to understand the forms as future perfects (“for you, O Lord, will have helped me and comforted me”).

1.note: sn Grant the king…Grant the king’s son. It is not entirely clear whether v. 1 envisions one individual or two. The phrase “the king’s son” in the second line may simply refer to “the king” of the first line, drawing attention to the fact that he has inherited his dynastic rule. Another option is that v. 1 envisions a co-regency between father and son (a common phenomenon in ancient Israel) or simply expresses a hope for a dynasty that champions justice.

1.anchor: the ability to make fair decisions!
1.note: tn Heb “and your justice to [the] son of [the] king.”

2.note: sn These people are called God’s oppressed ones because he is their defender (see Pss 9:12, 18; 10:12; 12:5).

3.anchor: and the hills will announce justice.
3.note: tn Heb “[the] mountains will bear peace to the people, and [the] hills with justice.” The personified mountains and hills probably represent messengers who will sweep over the land announcing the king’s just decrees and policies. See Isa 52:7 and C. A. Briggs and E. G. Briggs, Psalms (ICC), 2:133.

4.note: tn The prefixed verbal form appears to be an imperfect, not a jussive.
tn Heb “sons.”

5.anchor: for generation after generation.
5.note: tn Heb “with [the] sun, and before [the] moon [for] a generation, generations.” The rare expression דּוֹר דּוֹרִים (dor dorim, “generation, generations”) occurs only here, in Ps 102:24, and in Isa 51:8.

6.note: tc The form in the Hebrew text appears to be an otherwise unattested noun. Many prefer to emend the form to a verb from the root זָרַף (zaraf). BHS in textual note b on this verse suggests a Hiphil imperfect, third masculine plural יַזְרִיפוּ (yazrifu), while HALOT 283 s.v. *זרף prefers a Pilpel perfect, third masculine plural זִרְזְפוּ (zirzÿfu). The translation assumes the latter.
sn The imagery of this verse compares the blessings produced by the king’s reign to fructifying rains that cause the crops to grow.

7.anchor: peace will prevail as long as the moon remains in the sky.
7.note: tn Heb “and [there will be an] abundance of peace until there is no more moon.”

8.note: tn Heb “the river,” a reference to the Euphrates.

9.anchor: and his enemies will lick the dust.
9.note: sn As they bow down before him, it will appear that his enemies are licking the dust.

10.note: sn Sheba was located in Arabia.
sn Seba was located in Africa.

12.anchor: and the oppressed
12.note: tn The singular is representative. The typical oppressed individual here represents the entire group.

13.note: tn The prefixed verb form is best understood as a defectively written imperfect (see Deut 7:16).

14.note: tn Heb “their blood will be precious in his eyes.”

15.anchor: May they pronounce blessings on him all day long!
15.note: tn As in the preceding line, the prefixed verbal forms are understood as jussives with a grammatically indefinite subject (“and may one pray…and may one bless”). Of course, the king’s subjects, mentioned in the preceding context, are in view here.

16.anchor: May its crops
16.note: tc The MT has “from the city.” The translation assumes an emendation to עֲמִיר (’amir, “crops”).

16.anchor: be as abundant
16.note: tn The translation assumes that the verb צוץ (“flourish”) goes with the preceding line. The words “be as abundant” are supplied in the translation for clarification.

16.anchor: as the grass of the earth!
16.note: tc The traditional accentuation and vocalization of the MT differ from the text assumed by the present translation. The MT reads as follows: “May there be an abundance of grain in the earth, / and on the tops of the mountains! / May its [or “his”?] fruit [trees?] rustle like [the trees of] Lebanon! / May they flourish from the city, like the grass of the earth!” If one follows the MT, then it would appear that the “fruit” of the third line is a metaphorical reference to the king’s people, who flow out from the cities to populate the land (see line 4). Elsewhere in the OT people are sometimes compared to grass that sprouts up from the land (see v. 7, as well as Isa 27:6; Pss 92:7; 103:15). The translation understands a different poetic structural arrangement and, assuming the emendations mentioned in earlier notes, interprets each line of the verse to be a prayer for agricultural abundance.

17.anchor: May all nations consider him to be favored by God!
17.note: tn Heb “all the nations, may they regard him as happy.” The Piel is used here in a delocutive sense (“regard as”).

18.note: tn Heb “[the] one who does amazing things by himself.”

19.note: tn Heb “surely and surely” (אָמֵן וְאָמֵן [’amen vÿ’amen], i.e., “Amen and amen”). This is probably a congregational response of agreement to the immediately preceding statement about the propriety of praising God.

20.anchor: This collection of the prayers of David son of Jesse ends here.
20.note: tn Heb “the prayers of David, son of Jesse, are concluded.” As noted earlier, v. 20 appears to be a remnant of an earlier collection of psalms or an earlier edition of the Psalter. In the present arrangement of the Book of Psalms, not all psalms prior to this are attributed to David (see Pss 1-2, 10, 33, 42-50, 66-67, 71-72) and several psalms attributed to David appear after this (see Pss 86, 101, 103, 108-110, 122, 124, 131, 138-145).

1.anchor: ’s city is in the holy hills.
1.note: tn Heb “his foundation [is] in the hills of holiness.” The expression “his foundation” refers here by metonymy to the Lord’s dwelling place in Zion. The “hills” are the ones surrounding Zion (see Pss 125:2; 133:3).

3.anchor: People say wonderful things about you,
3.note: tn Heb “glorious things are spoken about you.” The translation assumes this is a general reference to compliments paid to Zion by those who live within her walls and by those who live in the surrounding areas and lands. Another option is that this refers to a prophetic oracle about the city’s glorious future. In this case one could translate, “wonderful things are announced concerning you.”

4.anchor: It is said of them, “This one was born there.”
4.note: tn Heb “and this one was born there.” The words “It is said of them” are not in the Hebrew text, but are supplied for clarification and stylistic purposes (see v. 5). Those advocating the universalistic interpretation understand “there” as referring to Zion, but it seems more likely that the adverb refers to the nations just mentioned. The foreigners are identified by their native lands.

5.anchor: and the sovereign One
5.note: tn Traditionally “Most High.”

5.anchor: makes her secure.”
5.note: tn Heb “and he makes her secure, the Most High.”

6.note: tn As noted in v. 4, the translation assumes a contrast between “there” (the various foreign lands) and “in her” (Zion). In contrast to foreigners, the citizens of Zion have special status because of their birthplace (v. 5). In this case vv. 4 and 6 form a structural frame around v. 5.

7.anchor: all of them sing within your walls.
7.note: tc Heb “and singers, like pipers, all my springs [are] in you.” The participial form חֹלְלִים (kholÿlim) appears to be from a denominative verb meaning “play the pipe,” though some derive the form from חוּל (khul, “dance”). In this case the duplicated lamed (ל) requires an emendation to מְחֹלְלִים (mÿkholÿlim, “a Polel form). The words are addressed to Zion. As it stands, the Hebrew text makes little, if any, sense. “Springs” are often taken here as a symbol of divine blessing and life”), but this reading does not relate to the preceding line in any apparent way. The present translation assumes an emendation of כָּל־מַעְיָנַי (kol-ma’yanay, “all my springs”) to כֻּלָּם עָנוּ (kullam ’anu, “all of them sing,” with the form עָנוּ being derived from עָנָה, ’anah, “sing”).

1.anchor: O Lord, you have been our protector
1.note: tn Or “place of safety.” See Ps 71:3.

2.note: tn Heb “and from everlasting to everlasting you [are] God.” Instead of אֵל (’el, “God”) the LXX reads אַל (’al, “not”) and joins the negative particle to the following verse, making the verb תָּשֵׁב (tashev) a jussive. In this case v. 3a reads as a prayer, “do not turn man back to a low place.” However, taking תָּשֵׁב as a jussive is problematic in light of the following following wayyiqtol form וַתֹּאמֶר (vato’mer, “and you said/say”).

3.note: tn In this context the shortened prefix form does not function as a preterite, but indicates what is typical of the world.

tn The Hebrew term דַּכָּא (daka’) carries the basic sense of “crushed.” Elsewhere it refers to those who are “crushed” in spirit or contrite of heart (see Ps 34:18; Isa 57:15). If one understands this nuance here, then v. 3 is observing that God leads mankind to repentance (the term שׁוּב, shuv, “return,” which appears twice in this verse, is sometimes used of repentance.) However, the following context laments mankind’s mortality and the brevity of life, so it is doubtful if v. 3 should be understood so positively. It is more likely that דַּכָּא here refers to “crushed matter,” that is, the dust that fills the grave (see HALOT 221 s.v. s.v. I דַּכָּא; BDB 194 s.v. דַּכָּא). In this case one may hear an echo of Gen 3:19.

4.anchor: or like one of the divisions of the nighttime.
4.note: sn The divisions of the nighttime. The ancient Israelites divided the night into distinct periods, or “watches.”

5.anchor: You bring their lives to an end and they “fall asleep.”
5.note: tn Heb “you bring them to an end [with] sleep.” The Hebrew verb זָרַם (zaram) has traditionally been taken to mean “flood” or “overwhelm” (note the Polel form of a root זרם in Ps 77:17, where the verb is used of the clouds pouring down rain). However, the verb form here is Qal, not Polel, and is better understood as a homonym meaning “to make an end [of life].” The term שֵׁנָה (shenah, “sleep”) can be taken as an adverbial accusative; it is a euphemism here for death (see Ps 76:5-6).

6.note: tn The Polel form of this verb occurs only here. Perhaps the form should be emended to a Qal (which necessitates eliminating the final lamed [ל] as dittographic). See Ps 37:2.

7.note: tn Or “for.”

8.anchor: you even know about our hidden sins.
8.note: tn Heb “what we have hidden to the light of your face.” God’s face is compared to a light or lamp that exposes the darkness around it.

9.anchor: the years of our lives pass quickly, like a sigh.
9.note: tn Heb “we finish our years like a sigh.” In Ezek 2:10 the word הֶגֶה (hegeh) elsewhere refers to a grumbling or moaning sound. Here a brief sigh or moan is probably in view. If so, the simile pictures one’s lifetime as transient. Another option is that the simile alludes to the weakness that characteristically overtakes a person at the end of one’s lifetime. In this case the phrase could be translated, “we end our lives with a painful moan.”

10.note: tn or “for.”
tn Heb “it passes quickly.” The subject of the verb is probably “their pride” (see the preceding line). The verb גּוּז (guz) means “to pass” here; it occurs only here and in Num 11:31.
sn We fly away. The psalmist compares life to a bird that quickly flies off (see Job 20:8).

11.anchor: Your raging fury causes people to fear you.
11.note: tn Heb “and like your fear [is] your raging fury.” Perhaps one should emend וּכְיִרְאָתְךְ (ukhyir’otekh, “and like your fear”) to יִרְאָתְךְ (yir’otkh, “your fear”), understanding a virtual dittography (אַפֶּךָ וּכְיִרְאָתְךְ, ’apekha ukhyir’otekh) to have occurred. In this case the psalmist asserts “your fear [is] your raging fury,” that is, your raging fury is what causes others to fear you. The suffix on “fear” is understood as objective.

12.anchor: so that we might live wisely.
12.note: tn Heb “and we will bring a heart of wisdom.” After the imperative of the preceding line, the prefixed verbal form with the conjunction indicates purpose/result. The Hebrew term “heart” here refers to the center of one’s thoughts, volition, and moral character.

13.note: tn Elsewhere the Niphal of נָחַם (nakham) + the preposition עַל (’al) + a personal object has the nuance “be comforted concerning [the personal object’s death]” (see 2 Sam 13:39; Jer 31:15). However, here the context seems to demand “feel sorrow for,” “have pity on.” In Deut 32:36 and Ps 135:14, where “servants” is also the object of the preposition, this idea is expressed with the Hitpael form of the verb.

14.anchor: Then we will shout for joy and be happy
14.note: tn After the imperative (see the preceding line) the cohortatives with the prefixed conjunction indicate purpose/result.

15.anchor: in proportion to the years we have experienced
15.note: tn Heb “have seen.”

16.anchor: May their sons see your majesty!
16.note: tn Heb “and your majesty to their sons.” The verb “be revealed” is understood by ellipsis in the second line.

17.anchor: Yes, make them successful!
17.note: tn Heb “and the work of our hands establish over us, and the work of our hands, establish it.”

1.anchor: and resides in the protective shadow
1.note: sn The Lord is compared here to a bird who protects its young under the shadow of its wings (see v. 4).

sn The divine name used here is “Shaddai” (שַׁדַּי, shadday; see also Ps 68:14). Shaddai (or El Shaddai) is the mighty king (sovereign judge) of the world who grants life/blesses and kills/judges. In Genesis he blesses the patriarchs with fertility and promises numerous descendants. Outside Genesis he both blesses/protects and takes away life/happiness.

3.anchor: he will certainly rescue you from the snare of the hunter
3.note: tn The word refers specifically to a fowler (or hunter of birds).

4.anchor: His faithfulness is like a shield or a protective wall.
4.note: tn Traditionally the Hebrew term סֹחֵרָה (sokherah), which occurs only here in the OT, has been understood to refer to a buckler or small shield (see BDB 695 s.v.). But HALOT 750 s.v., on the basis of evidence from the cognate languages, proposes the meaning “wall.”

5.anchor: You need not fear the terrors of the night,
5.note: tn This probably alludes to a sneak attack by enemies in the darkness of night (see Song 3:8).

6.anchor: or the disease that comes at noon.
6.note: sn As in Deut 32:23-24, vv. 5-6 closely associate military attack and deadly disease. Perhaps the latter alludes to one of the effects of siege warfare on the population of an entrapped city, which was especially vulnerable to the outbreak of epidemics.

7.note: tn Apparently the deadly disease mentioned in v. 6b is the understood subject here.

8.anchor: you will see the wicked paid back.
8.note: tn Heb “retribution on the wicked.”

9.note: tn Heb “for you, the Lord, my shelter, the Most High, you have made your dwelling place.”

10.note: tn For this sense of the Hebrew term נגע see Ps 38:11.
tn Heb “your tent.”

11.anchor: to protect you in all you do.
11.note: tn Heb “in all your ways.”

12.anchor: so you will not slip and fall on a stone.
12.note: tn Heb “so your foot will not strike a stone.”

13.note: tn Heb “walk upon.”
tn Or perhaps “cobra” (see Ps 58:4).

14.note: tn Or “make him secure” (Heb “set him on high”).

14.anchor: because he is loyal to me.
14.note: tn Heb “because he knows my name” (see Ps 9:10).

16.anchor: I will satisfy him with long life,
16.note: tn Heb “length of days.”

1.anchor: he wears strength around his waist.
1.note: sn Strength is compared here to a belt that one wears for support. The Lord’s power undergirds his rule.

2.note: tn Heb “from antiquity [are] you.” As the context suggests, this refers specifically to God’s royal position, not his personal existence.

3.note: tn Heb “the waves lift up, O Lord, the waves lift up their voice, the waves lift up their crashing.”

4.note: tn Heb “mighty on high [is] the Lord.”

5.anchor: Holiness
5.note: sn Holiness refers here to God’s royal transcendence (see vv. 1-4), as well as his moral authority and perfection (see v. 5a).

5.anchor: , forever.
5.note: tn Heb “for your house holiness is fitting, O Lord, for length of days.”

1.anchor: and at night I pray before you.
1.note: tn Heb “[by] day I cry out, in the night before you.”

2.note: tn Heb “turn your ear.”

3.anchor: and I am ready to enter Sheol.
3.note: tn Heb “and my life approaches Sheol.”

4.anchor: I am like a helpless man,
4.note: tn Heb “I am like a man [for whom] there is no help.”

5.anchor: and who are cut off from your power.
5.note: tn Heb “from your hand.”

6.anchor: You place me in the lowest regions of the pit,
6.note: tn The noun בּוֹר (bor, “pit,” “cistern”) is sometimes used of the grave and/or the realm of the dead. See v. 4.

8.anchor: I am trapped and cannot get free.
8.note: tn Heb “[I am] confined and I cannot go out.”

9.anchor: I spread out my hands in prayer to you.
9.note: tn Heb “I spread out my hands to you.” Spreading out the hands toward God was a prayer gesture (see Exod 9:29, 33; 1 Kgs 8:22, 38; 2 Chr 6:12-13, 29; Ezra 9:15; Job 11:13; Isa 1:15). The words “in prayer” have been supplied in the translation to clarify this.

10.note: tn Heb “Rephaim,” a term that refers to those who occupy the land of the dead (see Isa 14:9; 26:14, 19).

11.anchor: or your faithfulness in the place of the dead?
11.note: tn Heb “in Abaddon,” a name for Sheol. The noun is derived from a verbal root meaning “to perish,” “to die.”

12.anchor: or your deliverance in the land of oblivion?
12.note: tn Heb “forgetfulness.” The noun, which occurs only here in the OT, is derived from a verbal root meaning “to forget.”sn The rhetorical questions in vv. 10-12 expect the answer, “Of course not!”

14.anchor: and pay no attention to me?
14.note: tn Heb “[why] do you hide your face from me?”

15.anchor: I have been subjected to your horrors and am numb with pain.
15.note: tn Heb “I carry your horrors [?].” The meaning of the Hebrew form אָפוּנָה (’afunah), which occurs only here in the OT, is unclear. It may be an adverb meaning “very much” (BDB 67 s.v.), though some prefer to emend the text to אָפוּגָה (’afugah, “I am numb”) from the verb פוּג (pug; see Pss 38:8; 77:2).

16.anchor: Your anger overwhelms me;
16.note: tn Heb “passes over me.”

17.anchor: they join forces and encircle me.
17.note: tn Heb “they encircle me together.”

18.anchor: those who know me leave me alone in the darkness.
18.note: tn Heb “those known by me, darkness.”

1.anchor: and to sing praises to your name, O sovereign One!
1.note: tn Traditionally “O Most High.”

2.note: tn The words “it is fitting” are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons. Verses 1-3 are actually one long sentence in the Hebrew text, but this has been divided up into two shorter sentences in the translation in keeping with contemporary English style.

4.anchor: I will sing for joy because of what you have done.
4.note: tn Heb “the works of your hands.”

5.anchor: Your plans are very intricate!
5.note: tn Heb “very deep [are] your thoughts.” God’s “thoughts” refer here to his moral design of the world, as outlined in vv. 6-15.

6.anchor: the fool does not understand this.
6.note: tn Heb “the brutish man does not know, and the fool does not understand this.” The adjective בַּעַר (ba’ar, “brutish”) refers to spiritual insensitivity, not mere lack of intelligence or reasoning ability (see Pss 49:10; 73:22; Prov 12:1; 30:2, as well as the use of the related verb in Ps 94:8).

7.anchor: it is so that they may be annihilated.
7.note: tn Heb “in order that they might be destroyed permanently.”sn God allows the wicked to prosper temporarily so that he might reveal his justice. When the wicked are annihilated, God demonstrates that wickedness does not pay off.

8.note: tn Heb “[are elevated] on high.”

9.note: tn Or “for.”

10.note: tn The Hebrew verb בָּלַל (balal) usually has the nuance “to mix.” Here it seems to mean “to smear” or “to anoint.” Some emend the form to בַּלֹּתַנִי (ballotaniy; a second person form of the verb with a first person suffix) and read, “you anoint me.”

11.anchor: I hear the defeated cries of the evil foes who attacked me.
11.note: tn Heb “those who rise up against me, evil [foes], my ears hear.”

12.anchor: they grow high like a cedar in Lebanon.
12.note: sn The cedars of the Lebanon forest were well-known in ancient Israel for their immense size.

14.anchor: they are filled with vitality and have many leaves.
14.note: tn Heb “they are juicy and fresh.”

15.anchor: is just and never unfair.
15.note: tn Heb “so that [they] proclaim that upright [is] the Lord, my rocky summit, and there is no injustice in him.”

1.anchor: to future generations I will proclaim your faithfulness.
1.note: tn Heb “to a generation and a generation I will make known your faithfulness with my mouth.”

2.anchor: in the skies you set up your faithfulness.”
2.note: sn You set up your faithfulness. This may allude to the Lord’s heavenly throne, which symbolizes his just rule and from which the Lord decrees his unconditional promises (see vv. 8, 14).

3.note: tn The words “the Lord said” are supplied in the translation for clarification. It is clear that the words of vv. 3-4 are spoken by the Lord, in contrast to vv. 1-2, which are spoken by the psalmist.

4.anchor: and establish your throne throughout future generations.’”
4.note: tn Heb “and I will build to a generation and a generation your throne.”

5.anchor: as well as your faithfulness in the angelic assembly.
5.note: tn Heb “in the assembly of the holy ones.” The phrase “holy ones” sometimes refers to God’s people (Ps 34:9) or to their priestly leaders (2 Chr 35:3), but here it refers to God’s heavenly assembly and the angels that surround his throne (see vv. 6-7).

6.note: tn Heb “sons of gods”; or “sons of God.” Though אֵלִים (’elim) is vocalized as a plural form (“gods”) in the Hebrew text, it is likely that the final mem (ם) is actually enclitic rather than a plural marker. In this case one may read “God.” Some, following a Qumran text and the LXX, also propose the phrase occurred in the original text of Deut 32:8. The phrase בְנֵי אֵלִים (vÿney ’elim, “sons of gods” or “sons of God”) occurs only here and in Ps 29:1. Since the “sons of gods/God” are here associated with “the assembly of the holy ones” and “council of the holy ones,” the heavenly assembly (comprised of so-called “angels” and other supernatural beings) appears to be in view. See Job 5:1; 15:15 and Zech 14:5, where these supernatural beings are referred to as “holy ones.” In Canaanite mythological texts the divine council of the high god El is called “the sons of El.” The OT apparently uses the Canaanite phrase, applying it to the supernatural beings that surround the Lord’s heavenly throne.

7.note: tn Or perhaps “feared by.”

8.note: tn Traditionally “God of hosts.” The title here pictures the Lord as enthroned in the midst of the angelic hosts of heaven.

9.note: tn Heb “rise up.”

10.note: tn Heb “Rahab.” The name “Rahab” means “proud one.” Since it is sometimes used of Egypt (see Ps 87:4; Isa 30:7), the passage may allude to the exodus. However, the name is also used of the sea (or the mythological sea creature) which symbolizes the disruptive forces of the world that seek to replace order with chaos (see Job 9:13; 26:12). Isa 51:9 appears to combine the mythological and historical referents. The association of Rahab with the sea in Ps 89 (see v. 9) suggests that the name carries symbolic force in this context. In this case the passage may allude to creation (see vv. 11-12), when God overcame the great deep and brought order out of chaos.
tn Heb “like one fatally wounded.”

11.anchor: You made the world and all it contains.
11.note: tn Heb “the world and its fullness, you established them.”

12.anchor: Tabor and Hermon
12.note: sn Tabor and Hermon were two of the most prominent mountains in Palestine.

13.note: sn The Lord’s arm, hand, and right hand all symbolize his activities, especially his exploits in war.
tn Heb “is lifted up.” The idiom “the right hand is lifted up” refers to victorious military deeds (see Pss 89:42; 118:16).

14.anchor: Loyal love and faithfulness characterize your rule.
14.note: tn Heb “are in front of your face.” The idiom can mean “confront” (Ps 17:13) or “meet, enter the presence of” (Ps 95:2).

15.anchor: , they experience your favor.
15.note: tn Heb “in the light of your face they walk.” The idiom “light of your face” probably refers to a smile (see Eccl 8:1), which in turn suggests favor and blessing (see Num 6:25; Pss 4:6; 31:16; 44:3; 67:1; 80:3, 7, 19; Dan 9:17).

16.anchor: and are vindicated
16.note: tn Heb “are lifted up.”

17.anchor: By your favor we are victorious.
17.note: tn Heb “you lift up our horn,” or if one follows the marginal reading (Qere), “our horn is lifted up.” The horn of an ox underlies the metaphor (see Deut 33:17; 1 Kgs 22:11; Ps 92:10). The horn of the wild ox is frequently a metaphor for military strength; the idiom “exalt/lift up the horn” signifies military victory (see 1 Sam 2:10; Pss 75:10; 89:24; 92:10; Lam 2:17).

18.anchor: our king to the Holy One of Israel.
18.note: sn The basic sense of the word “holy” is “set apart from that which is commonplace, special, unique.” The Lord’s holiness is first and foremost his transcendent sovereignty as the ruler of the world. He is “set apart” from the world over which he rules. At the same time his holiness encompasses his moral authority, which derives from his royal position. As king he has the right to dictate to his subjects how they are to live; indeed his very own character sets the standard for proper behavior. This expression is a common title for the Lord in the book of Isaiah.

19.anchor: I have raised up a young man
19.note: tn Or perhaps “a chosen one.”

20.anchor: With my holy oil I have anointed him as king.
20.note: tn The words “as king” are supplied in the translation for clarification, indicating that a royal anointing is in view.

21.anchor: My hand will support him,
21.note: tn Heb “with whom my hand will be firm.”

22.anchor: a violent oppressor will not be able to humiliate him.
22.note: tn Heb “and a son of violence will not oppress him.” The imperfect is understood in a modal sense, indicating capability or potential. The reference to a “son of violence” echoes the language of God’s promise to David in 2 Sam 7:10 (see also 1 Chr 17:9).

24.anchor: and by my name he will win victories.
24.note: tn Heb “and by my name his horn will be lifted up.” The horn of an ox underlies the metaphor (see Deut 33:17; 1 Kgs 22:11; Ps 92:10). The horn of the wild ox is frequently a metaphor for military strength; the idiom “exalt/lift up the horn” signifies military victory (see 1 Sam 2:10; Pss 75:10; 92:10; Lam 2:17).

25.anchor: his right hand over the rivers.
25.note: tn Some identify “the sea” as the Mediterranean and “the rivers” as the Euphrates and its tributaries. However, it is more likely that “the sea” and “the rivers” are symbols for hostile powers that oppose God and the king (see v. 9, as well as Ps 93:3-4).

26.anchor: ‘You are my father,
26.note: sn You are my father. The Davidic king was viewed as God’s “son” (see 2 Sam 7:14; Ps 2:7). The idiom reflects ancient Near Eastern adoption language associated with covenants of grant, by which a lord would reward a faithful subject by elevating him to special status, referred to as “sonship.” Like a son, the faithful subject received an “inheritance,” viewed as an unconditional, eternal gift. Such gifts usually took the form of land and/or an enduring dynasty. See M. Weinfeld, “The Covenant of Grant in the Old Testament and in the Ancient Near East,” JAOS 90 (1970): 184-203, for general discussion and some striking extra-biblical parallels.

26.anchor: my God, and the protector who delivers me.’
26.note: tn Heb “the rocky summit of my deliverance.”

27.anchor: I will appoint him to be my firstborn son,
27.note: sn The firstborn son typically had special status and received special privileges.

28.anchor: and my covenant with him is secure.
28.note: tn Heb “forever I will keep for him my loyal love and will make my covenant secure for him.”

29.anchor: and make his throne as enduring as the skies above.
29.note: tn Heb “and his throne like the days of the heavens.”

32.anchor: their sin by inflicting them with bruises.

33.anchor: nor be unfaithful to my promise.
33.note: tn Heb “and I will not deal falsely with my faithfulness.”

34.anchor: or go back on what I promised.
34.note: tn Heb “and what proceeds out of my lips I will not alter.”

36.anchor: His throne will endure before me, like the sun,
36.note: tn Heb “and his throne like the sun before me.”

37.anchor: his throne will endure like the skies.”
37.note: tn Heb “and a witness in the sky, secure.” Scholars have offered a variety of opinions as to the identity of the “witness” referred to here, none of which is very convincing. It is preferable to join וְעֵד (vÿ’ed) to עוֹלָם (’olam) in the preceding line and translate the commonly attested phrase עוֹלָם וְעֵד (“forever”). In this case one may translate the second line, “[it] will be secure like the skies.” Another option (the one reflected in the present translation) is to take עד as a rare noun meaning “throne” or “dais.” This noun is attested in Ugaritic; see, for example, CTA 16 vi 22-23, where ksi (= כִּסֵּא, kisse’, “throne”) and ’d (= עד, “dais”) appear as synonyms in the poetic parallelism (see G. R. Driver, Canaanite Myths and Legends, 91). Emending בַּשַּׁחַק (bashakhaq, “in the heavens”) to כַּשַׁחַק (kashakhaq, “like the heavens”) – bet/kaf (כ/ב) confusion is widely attested – one can then read “[his] throne like the heavens [is] firm/stable.” Verse 29 refers to the enduring nature of the heavens, while Job 37:18 speaks of God spreading out the heavens (שְׁחָקִים, shÿkhaqim) and compares their strength to a bronze mirror. Ps 89:29 uses the term שָׁמַיִם (shamayim, “skies”) which frequently appears in parallelism to שְׁחָקִים.

38.anchor: you are angry with your chosen king.
38.note: tn Heb “your anointed one.” The Hebrew phrase מְשִׁיחֶךָ (mÿshikhekha, “your anointed one”) refers here to the Davidic king (see Pss 2:2; 18:50; 20:6; 28:8; 84:9; 132:10, 17).

39.anchor: you have thrown his crown to the ground.
39.note: tn Heb “you dishonor [or “desecrate”] on the ground his crown.”

40.anchor: You have broken down all his
40.note: tn The king here represents the land and cities over which he rules.

41.note: tn Heb “all the passersby on the road.”

42.anchor: You have allowed his adversaries to be victorious,
42.note: tn Heb “you have lifted up the right hand of his adversaries.” The idiom “the right hand is lifted up” refers to victorious military deeds (see Pss 89:13; 118:16).

43.anchor: and have not sustained him in battle.
43.note: tn Heb “and you have not caused him to stand in the battle.”

44.note: tn The Hebrew verb מָגַר (magar) occurs only here and perhaps in Ezek 21:17.

45.anchor: You have cut short his youth,
45.note: tn Heb “the days of his youth” (see as well Job 33:25).

46.note: tn Heb “How long, O Lord, will hide yourself forever?”

47.anchor: Why do you make all people so mortal?
47.note: tn Heb “For what emptiness do you create all the sons of mankind?” In this context the term שָׁוְא (shavah) refers to mankind’s mortal nature and the brevity of life (see vv. 45, 48).

48.anchor: or deliver his life from the power of Sheol.
48.note: tn Heb “Who [is] the man [who] can live and not see death, [who] can deliver his life from the hand of Sheol?” The rhetorical question anticipates the answer, “No one!”

49.anchor: the ones performed in accordance with your reliable oath to David?
49.note: tn Heb “[which] you swore on oath to David by your faithfulness.”

50.anchor: and of how I must bear so many insults from people!
50.note: tn Heb “my lifting up in my arms [or “against my chest”] all of the many, peoples.” The term רַבִּים (rabbim, “many”) makes no apparent sense here. For this reason some emend the text to רִבֵי (rivey, “attacks by”), a defectively written plural construct form of רִיב (riv, “dispute; quarrel”).

51.anchor: they insult your chosen king as they dog his footsteps.
51.note: tn Heb “[by] which your enemies, O Lord, taunt, [by] which they taunt [at] the heels of your anointed one.”

52.note: tn Heb “surely and surely” (אָמֵן וְאָמֵן [’amen vÿ’amen], i.e., “Amen and amen”). This is probably a congregational response to the immediately preceding statement about the propriety of praising God; thus it has been translated “We agree! We agree!”

1.note: tn The Hebrew verb נוּט (nut) occurs only here in the OT, but the meaning can be determined on the basis of the parallelism with רָגַז (ragaz, “tremble”) and evidence from the cognate languages (see H. R. Cohen, Biblical Hapax Legomena [SBLDS], 121).

2.note: tn Heb “great.”

3.note: tn The pronoun refers to the Lord himself (see vv. 5, 9).

4.anchor: You ensure that legal decisions will be made fairly;
4.note: tn Heb “you establish fairness.”

5.anchor: Worship
5.note: tn Or “bow down.”

6.note: tn Heb “those who.” The participle is in apposition to the phrase “those who called on his name” in the preceding line.

7.anchor: He spoke to them from a pillar of cloud;
7.note: sn A pillar of cloud. The psalmist refers to the reality described in Exod 33:9-10; Num 12:5; and Deut 31:15.

8.anchor: but also one who punished their sinful deeds.
8.note: tn Heb “a God of lifting up [i.e., forgiveness] you were to them, and an avenger concerning their deeds.” The present translation reflects the traditional interpretation, which understands the last line as qualifying the preceding one. God forgave Moses and Aaron, but he also disciplined them when they sinned (cf. NIV, NRSV). Another option is to take “their deeds” as referring to harmful deeds directed against Moses and Aaron. In this case the verse may be translated, “and one who avenged attacks against them.” Still another option is to emend the participial form נֹקֵם (noqem, “an avenger”) to נֹקָם (noqam), a rare Qal participial form of נָקַה (naqah, “purify”) with a suffixed pronoun. In this case one could translate, “and one who purified them from their [sinful] deeds” (cf. NEB “and held them innocent”).

9.note: tn Or “exalt.”

1.anchor: in the midst of the gods
1.note: sn The present translation assumes that the Hebrew term אֱלֹהִים (’elohim, “gods”) here refers to the pagan gods who supposedly comprise El’s assembly according to Canaanite religion. Those who reject the polemical view of the psalm prefer to see the referent as human judges or rulers (אֱלֹהִים sometimes refers to officials appointed by God, see Exod 21:6; 22:8-9; Ps 45:6) or as angelic beings (אֱלֹהִים sometimes refers to angelic beings, see Gen 3:5; Ps 8:5).
sn The picture of God rendering judgment among the gods clearly depicts his sovereign authority as universal king (see v. 8, where the psalmist boldly affirms this truth).

2.anchor: and show favoritism to the wicked?
2.note: tn Heb “and the face of the wicked lift up.”

3.anchor: Defend the cause of the poor and the fatherless!
3.note: tn The Hebrew noun יָתוֹם (yatom) refers to one who has lost his father (not necessarily his mother, see Ps 109:9). Because they were so vulnerable and were frequently exploited, fatherless children are often mentioned as epitomizing the oppressed (see Pss 10:14; 68:5; 94:6; 146:9; as well as Job 6:27; 22:9; 24:3, 9; 29:12; 31:17, 21).

4.note: tn Heb “hand.”

5.anchor: while all the foundations of the earth crumble.
5.note: sn These gods, though responsible for justice, neglect their duty. Their self-imposed ignorance (which the psalmist compares to stumbling around in the dark) results in widespread injustice, which threatens the social order of the world (the meaning of the phrase all the foundations of the earth crumble).

6.anchor: all of you are sons of the Most High.’
6.note: sn Normally in the OT the title Most High belongs to the God of Israel, but in this context, where the mythological overtones are so strong, it probably refers to the Canaanite high god El (see v. 1, as well as Isa 14:13).

7.anchor: you will fall like all the other rulers.”
7.note: tn Heb “like one of the rulers.” The comparison does not necessarily imply that they are not rulers. The expression “like one of” can sometimes mean “as one of” (Gen 49:16; Obad 11) or “as any other of” (Judg 16:7, 11).

8.note: tn The translation assumes that the Qal of נָחַל (nakhal) here means “to own; to possess,” and that the imperfect emphasizes a general truth. Another option is to translate the verb as future, “for you will take possession of all the nations” (cf. NIV “all the nations are your inheritance”).

1.note: tn Heb “his right hand delivers for him and his holy arm.” The right hand and arm symbolize his power as a warrior-king (see Isa 52:10). His arm is “holy” in the sense that it is in a category of its own; God’s power is incomparable.

2.note: tn Heb “makes known his deliverance.”

3.anchor: All the ends of the earth see our God deliver us.
3.note: tn Heb “the deliverance of our God,” with “God” being a subjective genitive (= God delivers).

9.note: tn The verbal forms in v. 9 probably describe God’s typical, characteristic behavior, though they may depict in dramatic fashion the outworking of divine judgment or anticipate a future judgment of worldwide proportions (“will judge…”).

1.anchor: O God who avenges, reveal your splendor!
1.note: tn Heb “shine forth” (see Pss 50:2; 80:1).

3.anchor: how long will the wicked celebrate?
3.note: tn Or “exult.”

4.note: tn The Hitpael of אָמַר (’amar) occurs only here (and perhaps in Isa 61:6).

5.anchor: they oppress the nation that belongs to you.
5.note: tn Or “your inheritance.”

6.note: tn The Hebrew noun יָתוֹם (yatom) refers to one who has lost his father (not necessarily his mother, see Ps 109:9). Because they were so vulnerable and were frequently exploited, fatherless children are often mentioned as epitomizing the oppressed (see Pss 10:14; 68:5; 82:3; 146:9; as well as Job 6:27; 22:9; 24:3, 9; 29:12; 31:17, 21).

7.anchor: the God of Jacob does not take notice of it.”
7.note: tn Heb “does not understand.”

8.note: tn Heb “understand.” The verb used in v. 7 is repeated here for rhetorical effect. The people referred to here claim God is ignorant of their actions, but the psalmist corrects their faulty viewpoint.
tn Heb “[you] brutish among the people.”

9.anchor: Does the one who forms the human eye not see?
9.note: tn Heb “The one who plants an ear, does he not hear? The one who forms an eye, does he not see?”

11.anchor: peoples’ thoughts are morally bankrupt.
11.note: tn Heb “the Lord knows the thoughts of man, that they are emptiness.” The psalmist thinks specifically of the “thoughts” expressed in v. 7.

12.anchor: How blessed is the one
12.note: tn Heb “[Oh] the happiness [of] the man.” Hebrew wisdom literature often assumes and reflects the male-oriented perspective of ancient Israelite society. The principle of the psalm is certainly applicable to all people, regardless of their gender or age. To facilitate modern application, we translate the gender and age specific “man” with the more neutral “one.” The generic masculine pronoun is used in v. 2.

13.note: tn Heb “until a pit is dug for the wicked.”

14.anchor: he does not abandon the nation that belongs to him.
14.note: tn Or “his inheritance.”

15.anchor: and all the morally upright
15.note: tn Heb “all the pure of heart.” The “heart” is here viewed as the seat of one’s moral character and motives. The “pure of heart” are God’s faithful followers who trust in and love the Lord and, as a result, experience his deliverance (see Pss 7:10; 11:2; 32:11; 36:10; 64:10; 97:11).

15.anchor: will be vindicated.
15.note: tn Heb “and after it [are] the pure of heart.”

16.anchor: Who will stand up for me against the evildoers?
16.note: sn Who will stand up for me…? The questions anticipate the answer, “No one except God” (see v. 17).

17.anchor: I would have laid down in the silence of death.
17.note: tn Heb “If the Lord [were] not my help, quickly my life would have lain down in silence.” The psalmist, perhaps speaking as the nation’s representative, recalls God’s past intervention. For other examples of conditional sentences with the term לוּלֵי (luley, “if not”) in the protasis and a perfect verbal form in the apodosis, see Pss 119:92 and 124:2-5.

19.anchor: your soothing touch makes me happy.
19.note: tn Heb “your comforts cause my soul to delight.”

20.note: tn Heb “Is a throne of destruction united to you, one that forms trouble upon a statute?” The rhetorical question anticipates the answer, “Of course not!” The translation, while not preserving the interrogative form of the statement, reflects its rhetorical force.

21.anchor: and condemn to death the innocent.
21.note: tn Heb “and the blood of the innocent they declare guilty.”

22.anchor: and my God will shelter me.
22.note: tn Heb “and my God [has become] a rocky summit of my safety.”

23.anchor: He will destroy them because of
23.note: tn Or “in.”

2.note: tn Heb “lift up.”

3.anchor: and on the day of the full moon when our festival begins.
3.note: tn Heb “at the full moon on the day of our festival.” The Hebrew word כֶּסֶה (keseh) is an alternate spelling of כֶּסֶא (kese’, “full moon”).sn The festival in view is probably the Feast of Tabernacles (Booths), which began on the fifteenth day of the seventh month when the moon was full. See Lev 23:34; Num 29:12.

4.anchor: For observing the festival is a requirement for Israel;
4.note: tn Heb “because a statute for Israel [is] it.”

5.anchor: I heard a voice I did not recognize.
5.note: tn Heb “a lip I did not know, I heard.” Here the term “lip” probably stands for speech or a voice. Apparently the psalmist speaks here and refers to God’s voice, whose speech is recorded in the following verses.

6.anchor: his hands were released from holding the basket.
6.note: sn I removed the burden. The Lord speaks metaphorically of how he delivered his people from Egyptian bondage. The reference to a basket/burden probably alludes to the hard labor of the Israelites in Egypt, where they had to carry loads of bricks (see Exod 1:14).

7.anchor: I tested you at the waters of Meribah.
7.note: sn The name Meribah means “strife.” Two separate but similar incidents at the place called Meribah are recorded in the Pentateuch (Exod 17:1-7; Num 20:1-13). In both cases the Israelites complained about lack of water and the Lord miraculously provided for them.

8.anchor: O Israel, if only you would obey me!
8.note: tn The Hebrew particle אִם (“if”) and following prefixed verbal form here express a wish (GKC 321 §109.b). Note that the apodosis (the “then” clause of the conditional sentence) is suppressed.

9.note: tn The imperfect verbal forms in v. 9 have a modal function, expressing what is obligatory.
tn Heb “different”; “illicit.”

11.anchor: Israel did not submit to me.
11.note: tn The Hebrew expression אָבָה לִי (’avah liy) means “submit to me” (see Deut 13:8).

12.anchor: they did what seemed right to them.
12.note: tn Heb “they walked in their counsel.” The prefixed verbal form is either preterite (“walked”) or a customary imperfect (“were walking”).

13.anchor: If only Israel would keep my commands!
13.note: tn Heb “[and if only] Israel would walk in my ways.”

14.note: tn Heb “turn my hand against.” The idiom “turn the hand against” has the nuance of “strike with the hand, attack” (see Isa 1:25; Ezek 38:12; Amos 1:8; Zech 13:7).

15.anchor: May they be permanently humiliated!)
15.note: tc Heb “and may their time be forever.” The Hebrew term עִתָּם (’ittam, “their time”) must refer here to the “time” of the demise and humiliation of those who hate the Lord. Some propose an emendation to בַּעֲתָתָם (ba’atatam) or בִּעֻתָם (bi’utam; “their terror”; i.e., “may their terror last forever”), but the omission of bet (ב) in the present Hebrew text is difficult to explain, making the proposed emendation unlikely.tn The verb form at the beginning of the line is jussive, indicating that this is a prayer. The translation assumes that v. 15 is a parenthetical “curse” offered by the psalmist. Having heard the reference to Israel’s enemies (v. 14), the psalmist inserts this prayer, reminding the Lord that they are God’s enemies as well.

16.anchor: and would satisfy your appetite
16.note: tn Heb “you.” The second person singular pronominal suffix refers to Israel, as in vv. 7-10.

16.anchor: with honey from the rocky cliffs.”
16.note: sn The language in this verse, particularly the references to wheat and honey, is reminiscent of Deut 32:13-14.

2.note: tn Or “serve.”

3.anchor: He made us and we belong to him;
3.note: tn The present translation (like most modern translations) follows the Qere (marginal reading), which reads literally, “and to him [are] we.” The Kethib (consonantal text) has “and not we.” The suffixed preposition לו (“to him”) was confused aurally with the negative particle לא because the two sound identical.

5.anchor: and he is faithful through all generations.
5.note: tn Heb “and to a generation and a generation [is] his faithfulness.”

2.anchor: I will conduct my business with integrity in the midst of my palace.
2.note: tn Heb “I will walk about in the integrity of my heart in the midst of my house.”

3.anchor: I will have no part of it.
3.note: tn Heb “it [i.e., the doing of evil deeds] does not cling to me.”

4.note: tn Heb “know.” The king will not willingly allow perverse individuals to remain in his royal court.

5.anchor: I will not tolerate anyone who has a cocky demeanor and an arrogant attitude.
5.note: tn Heb “[one who has] pride of eyes and wideness [i.e., arrogance] of heart, him I will not endure.”

6.anchor: Those who walk in the way of integrity will attend me.
6.note: tn Heb “one who walks in the way of integrity, he will minister to me.”

7.anchor: Liars will not be welcome in my presence.
7.note: tn Heb “one who speaks lies will not be established before my eyes.”

1.anchor: Pay attention to my cry for help!
1.note: tn Heb “and may my cry for help come to you.”

2.note: tn Heb “turn toward me your ear.”

3.anchor: and my bones are charred like a fireplace.
3.note: tn The Hebrew noun מוֹ־קֵד (mo-qed, “fireplace”) occurs only here, in Isa 33:14 (where it refers to the fire itself), and perhaps in Lev 6:2.

4.note: tn Heb “I forget.”
sn I am unable to eat food. During his time of mourning, the psalmist refrained from eating. In the following verse he describes metaphorically the physical effects of fasting.

5.anchor: my bones protrude from my skin.
5.note: tn Heb “from the sound of my groaning my bone[s] stick to my flesh.” The preposition at the beginning of the verse is causal; the phrase “sound of my groaning” is metonymic for the anxiety that causes the groaning. The point seems to be this: Anxiety (which causes the psalmist to groan) keeps him from eating (v. 4). This physical deprivation in turn makes him emaciated – he is turned to “skin and bones,” so to speak.

6.anchor: I am like a screech owl
6.note: tn The Hebrew term כוֹס (khos) refers to a bird (see Lev 11:17; Deut 14:16), probably a type of owl (cf. NIV, REB “owl”; NRSV “little owl”).
sn By comparing himself to a screech owl among the ruins, the psalmist may be highlighting his loneliness (see v. 7), though he may also be comparing his cries for help to the owl’s screech.

7.note: tn This probably refers to the psalmist’s inability to sleep. Another option is to translate, “I keep watch,” in which case it might refer to watching for a response from the Lord (see vv. 1-2).

8.anchor: those who mock me use my name in their curses.
8.note: tn Heb “by me they swear.” When the psalmist’s enemies call judgment down on others, they hold the psalmist up as a prime example of what they desire their enemies to become.

9.anchor: and mix my drink with my tears,
9.note: tn Heb “weeping.”

10.note: tn Or “for.”

11.anchor: My days are coming to an end,
11.note: tn Heb “my days [are] like an extended [or “lengthening”] shadow,” that is, like a late afternoon shadow made by the descending sun that will soon be swallowed up by complete darkness.

12.anchor: and your reputation endures.
12.note: tn Heb “and your remembrance [is] for a generation and a generation.”

13.anchor: You will rise up and have compassion on Zion.
13.note: tn The imperfect verbal forms are understood as expressing the psalmist’s confidence in God’s intervention. Another option is to take them as expressing the psalmist’s request or wish, “You, rise up and have compassion!”

14.note: tn The Poel of חָנַן (khanan) occurs only here and in Prov 14:21, where it refers to having compassion on the poor.
tn Heb “her dust,” probably referring to the dust of the city’s rubble.

15.anchor: and all the kings of the earth will respect
15.note: tn The verb “will fear” is understood by ellipsis in the second line (see the preceding line).

17.anchor: and does not reject
17.note: tn Heb “despise.”

17.anchor: their request.
17.note: tn The perfect verbal forms in vv. 16-17 are functioning as future perfects, indicating future actions that will precede the future developments described in v. 15.

18.note: tn The Hebrew text has simply “this,” referring to the anticipated divine intervention on behalf of Zion (vv. 13, 16-17). The referent has been specified in the translation for clarity.

19.note: tn The perfect verbal forms in v. 19 are functioning as future perfects, indicating future actions that will precede the future developments described in v. 18.

20.anchor: and to set free those condemned to die,
20.note: tn Heb “the sons of death.” The phrase “sons of death” (see also Ps 79:11) is idiomatic for those condemned to die.

21.anchor: and praise him
21.note: tn Heb “his praise.”

21.anchor: in Jerusalem,
21.note: map For location see Map5-B1; Map6-F3; Map7-E2; Map8-F2; Map10-B3; JP1-F4; JP2-F4; JP3-F4; JP4-F4.

22.note: tn “and the kingdoms to serve the Lord.”

23.anchor: He has taken away my strength in the middle of life;
23.note: tn Heb “he has afflicted my strength in the way.” The term “way” refers here to the course of the psalmist’s life, which appears to be ending prematurely (vv. 23b-24).

24.anchor: You endure through all generations.
24.note: tn Heb “in a generation of generations [are] your years.”

26.anchor: like clothes you will remove them and they will disappear.
26.note: tn The Hebrew verb חָלַף (khalaf) occurs twice in this line, once in the Hiphil (“you will remove them”) and once in the Qal (“they will disappear”). The repetition draws attention to the statement.

27.anchor: But you remain;
27.note: tn Heb “you [are] he,” or “you [are] the one.” The statement may echo the Lord’s affirmation “I am he” in Isa 41:4; 43:10, 13; 46:10; 48:12. In each of these passages the affirmation emphasizes the fact that the Lord transcends time limitations, the very point being made in Ps 102:27.

28.anchor: will live securely in your presence.”
28.note: tn Heb “before you will be established.”

1.anchor: With all that is within me, praise
1.note: tn The verb “praise” is understood by ellipsis in the second line (see the preceding line).

2.anchor: Do not forget all his kind deeds!
2.note: tn Or “his benefits” (see 2 Chr 32:25, where the noun is also used of kind deeds performed by the Lord).

3.note: tn This relatively rare noun refers to deadly diseases (see Deut 29:22; Jer 14:18; 16:4; 2 Chr 21:19).

4.note: tn Or “redeems.”
tn The Hebrew term שַׁחַת (shakhat, “pit”) is often used as a title for Sheol (see Pss 16:10; 30:9; 49:9; 55:24.

5.anchor: so your youth is renewed like an eagle’s.
5.note: sn The expression your youth is renewed like an eagle’s may allude to the phenomenon of molting, whereby the eagle grows new feathers.

6.anchor: and executes justice for all the oppressed.
6.note: tn Heb “the Lord does fairness, and [acts of] justice for all the oppressed.”

7.note: tn Heb “made known his ways.” God’s “ways” in this context are his protective and salvific acts in fulfillment of his promise (see also Deut 32:4; Pss 18:30; 67:2; 77:13 [note vv. 11-12, 14]; 138:5; 145:17).

8.note: tn Heb “slow to anger” (see Ps 86:15).
tn Heb “and great of loyal love” (see Ps 86:15).

9.note: tn The Hebrew verb נָטַר (natar) is usually taken to mean “to keep; to guard,” with “anger” being understood by ellipsis. The idiom “to guard anger” is then understood to mean “to remain angry” (see Lev 19:18; Jer 3:5, 12; Nah 1:2). However, it is possible that this is a homonymic root meaning “to be angry” (see HALOT 695 s.v. נטר).

10.anchor: he does not repay us as our misdeeds deserve.
10.note: tn Heb “and not according to our misdeeds does he repay us.”

11.anchor: so his loyal love towers
11.note: tn For this sense of the verb גָבַר (gavar), see L. C. Allen, Psalms 101-150 (WBC), 17, 19.

11.anchor: over his faithful followers.
11.note: tn Heb “those who fear him.”

12.anchor: so he removes the guilt of our rebellious actions
12.note: tn The Hebrew term פֶּשַׁע (pesha’, rebellious act”) is here used metonymically for the guilt such actions produce.

13.anchor: has compassion on his faithful followers.
13.note: tn Heb “those who fear him.”

14.note: tn Heb “remembers.”
tn Heb “we [are] clay.”

15.anchor: A person’s life is like grass.
15.note: tn Heb “[as for] mankind, like grass [are] his days.” The Hebrew noun אֱנוֹשׁ (’enosh) is used here generically of human beings. What is said is true of all mankind.

16.anchor: but when the hot wind
16.note: tn Heb “[the] wind.” The word “hot” is supplied in the translation for clarification.

17.anchor: and is faithful to their descendants,
17.note: tn Heb “and his righteousness to sons of sons.”

18.anchor: who are careful to obey his commands.
18.note: tn Heb “to those who remember his precepts to do them.”

19.note: tn Heb “his kingdom rules over all.”

20.note: tn Heb “[you] mighty ones of strength, doers of his word, by listening to the voice of his word.”

21.anchor: you servants of his who carry out his desires!
21.note: tn Heb “his attendants, doers of his desire.”

22.note: tn Heb “places.”

2.anchor: equity and justice are the foundation of his throne.
2.note: sn The Lord’s throne symbolizes his kingship.

3.note: tn Heb “all around.”

7.anchor: All the gods bow down before him.
7.note: tn The translation assumes that the prefixed verbal form in the first line is an imperfect (“are ashamed”) and that the ambiguous form in the third line is a perfect (“bow down”) because the psalmist appears to be describing the effect of the Lord’s mighty theophany on those who witness it (see vv. 5, 8). Another option is to take the prefixed form in the first line as a jussive (“let all who worship idols be ashamed”) and the ambiguous form in the third line as an imperative (“All you gods, bow down before him!”; cf. NIV).

8.note: tn Heb “daughters.” The term “daughters” refers to the cities of Judah surrounding Zion (see Ps 48:11 and H. Haag, TDOT 2:336).

9.note: tn Traditionally “Most High.”

10.anchor: he delivers them from the power
10.note: tn Heb “hand.”

11.anchor: the morally upright experience joy.
11.note: tn Heb “Light is planted for the godly, and for the upright of heart joy.” The translation assumes an emendation of זָרַע (zara’, “planted”) to זָרַח (zara’, “shines”) which collocates more naturally with “light.” “Light” here symbolizes the joy (note the following line) that accompanies deliverance and the outpouring of divine favor.

12.anchor: Give thanks to his holy name.
12.note: tn Heb “to his holy remembrance.” The Hebrew noun זָכַר (zakhar, “remembrance”) here refers to the name of the Lord as invoked in liturgy and praise. Cf. Pss 6:5; 30:4. The Lord’s “name” is “holy” in the sense that it is a reminder of his uniqueness and greatness.

1.anchor: Let’s shout out praises to our protector who delivers us!
1.note: tn Heb “to the rocky summit of our deliverance.”

2.anchor: Let’s shout out to him in celebration!
2.note: tn Heb “with songs of joy.”

3.anchor: a great king who is superior to
3.note: tn Heb “above.”

4.anchor: The depths of the earth are in his hand,
4.note: tn The phrase “in his hand” means within the sphere of his authority.

6.anchor: Come! Let’s bow down and worship!
6.note: tn Heb “kneel down.”

7.anchor: Today, if only you would obey him!
7.note: tn Heb “if only you would listen to his voice.” The Hebrew particle אִם (’im, “if”) and following prefixed verbal form here express a wish (cf. Ps 81:8). Note that the apodosis (the “then” clause of the conditional sentence) is suppressed.

8.anchor: like they were that day at Massah
8.note: sn The name Massah means “testing.” This was another name (along with Meribah) given to the place where Israel complained following the Red Sea Crossing (see Exod 17:1-7, as well as Deut 6:16; 9:22; 33:8).
tn Heb “do not harden your heart[s] as [at] Meribah, as [in] the day of Massah in the wilderness.”

9.anchor: where your ancestors challenged my authority,
9.note: tn Heb “where your fathers tested me.”

10.anchor: they do not obey my commands.’
10.note: tn Heb “and they do not know my ways.” In this context the Lord’s “ways” are his commands, viewed as a pathway from which his people, likened to wayward sheep (see v. 7), wander.

11.anchor: ‘They will never enter into the resting place I had set aside for them.’”
11.note: tn Heb “my resting place.” The promised land of Canaan is here viewed metaphorically as a place of rest for God’s people, who are compared to sheep (see v. 7).

1.anchor: I will sing and praise you with my whole heart.
1.note: tn Heb “also my glory,” but this makes little sense in the context. Some view the term כָּבוֹד (“glory”) here as a metonymy for man’s inner being (see BDB 459 s.v. II כָּבוֹד 5), but it is preferable to emend the form to כְּבֵדִי (kÿvodiy, “my liver”). Like the heart, the liver is viewed as the seat of one’s emotions. See also Pss 16:9; 30:12; 57:9; as well as H. W. Wolff, Anthropology of the Old Testament, 64, and M. Dahood, Psalms (AB), 3:93. For an Ugaritic example of the heart/liver as the source of joy, see G. R. Driver, Canaanite Myths and Legends, 47-48: “her [Anat’s] liver swelled with laughter, her heart was filled with joy, the liver of Anat with triumph.”

2.anchor: I will wake up at dawn!
2.note: tn BDB 1007 s.v. שַׁחַר takes “dawn” as an adverbial accusative, though others understand it as a personified direct object. “Dawn” is used metaphorically for the time of deliverance and vindication the psalmist anticipates. When salvation “dawns,” the psalmist will “wake up” in praise.

3.anchor: I will sing praises to you before foreigners!
3.note: tn Or “the peoples.”

4.anchor: For your loyal love extends beyond the sky,
4.note: tn Heb “for great upon the sky [or “heavens”] [is] your loyal love.”

5.anchor: May your splendor cover the whole earth!
5.note: tn Heb “over all the earth [be] your splendor.” Though no verb appears, the tone of the statement is a prayer or wish. (Note the imperative form in the preceding line.)

6.anchor: so that the ones you love may be safe.
6.note: tn Or “may be rescued.” The lines are actually reversed in the Hebrew text: “So that the ones you love may be rescued, deliver by your power and answer me.”

7.anchor: the valley of Succoth I will measure off.
7.note: sn Shechem stands for the territory west of the Jordan River; the valley of Succoth represents the region east of the Jordan.

8.note: sn Judah, like Ephraim, was the other major tribe west of the Jordan River. The Davidic king, symbolized here by the royal scepter, came from this tribe.

9.anchor: I will make Edom serve me.
9.note: tn Heb “over Edom I will throw my sandal.” The point of the metaphor is not entirely clear. Some interpret this as idiomatic for “taking possession of.” Others translate עַל (’al) as “to” and understand this as referring to a master throwing his dirty sandal to a servant so that the latter might dust it off.

10.anchor: Who will bring me to Edom?
10.note: sn The psalmist speaks again and acknowledges his need for help in battle. He hopes God will volunteer, based on the affirmation of sovereignty over Edom in v. 9, but he is also aware that God has seemingly rejected the nation of Israel (v. 11).

12.anchor: for any help men might offer is futile.
12.note: tn Heb “and futile [is] the deliverance of man.”

13.note: sn On the expression trample down our enemies see Ps 44:5.

1.note: sn A new song is appropriate because the Lord is constantly intervening in the world as its just king. See also Pss 33:3; 40:3; 98:1.

2.anchor: Announce every day how he delivers!
2.note: tn Heb “announce from day to day his deliverance.”

3.note: tn The verb “tell” is understood by ellipsis (note the preceding line).

4.anchor: he is more awesome than all gods.
4.note: tn Or perhaps “and feared by all gods.” See Ps 89:7.

5.anchor: For all the gods of the nations are worthless,
5.note: tn The Hebrew term אֱלִילִים (’elilim, “worthless”) sounds like אֱלֹהִים (’elohim, “gods”). The sound play draws attention to the statement.

6.anchor: his sanctuary is firmly established and beautiful.
6.note: tn Heb “strength and beauty [are] in his sanctuary.”

8.note: tn Heb “the splendor of [i.e., “due”] his name.”

9.note: tn Or “in holy splendor.”

13.anchor: and the nations in accordance with his justice.
13.note: tn Heb “and the nations with his integrity.”

1.note: tn To sit at the “right hand” of the king was an honor (see 1 Kgs 2:19). In Ugaritic myth (CTA 4 v. 108-10) the artisan god Kothar-and Khasis is described as sitting at the right hand of the storm god Baal. See G. R. Driver, Canaanite Myths and Legends, 61-62.sn The Lord’s invitation to the Davidic king to sit down at his right hand reflects the king’s position as the Lord’s vice-regent.

1.anchor: until I make your enemies your footstool!”
1.note: sn When the Lord made his covenant with David, he promised to subdue the king’s enemies (see 2 Sam 7:9-11; Ps 89:22-23).

2.note: tn Since the Lord is mentioned in the third person (note the use of the first person in v. 1), it is likely that these are the psalmist’s words to the king, not a continuation of the oracle per se.
tn The prefixed verbal form is understood here as descriptive-dramatic or as generalizing, though it could be taken as future.
tn Heb “your strong scepter,” symbolic of the king’s royal authority and dominion.

3.note: tc Heb “in splendor of holiness.” The plural construct form הַדְרֵי (hadrey, from הָדַר, hadar, “splendor”) occurs only here; it may indicate degree or perhaps refer by metonymy to garments (see Pss 29:2 and 96:9, where the phrase הַדְרַת קֹדֶשׁ [hadrat qodesh] refers to “holy attire”). If one retains the reading of the MT, this phrase should probably be taken with the preceding line. However, because of the subsequent references to “dawn” and to “dew,” it is better to emend the text to הַרְרֵי קֹדֶשׁ (harrey qodesh, “mountains of holiness”), a reading found in many medieval Hebrew mss and in some other ancient witnesses (see Joel 2:2; Ps 133:3, as well as L. C. Allen, Psalms 101-150 [WBC], 80). The “mountains of holiness” are probably the hills surrounding Zion (see Ps 87:1; 125:2; 133:3).

tn Heb “from the womb of dawn.” The Hebrew noun רֶחֶם (rekhem, “womb”) is probably used here metonymically for “birth.” The form מִשְׁחָר (mishkhar) occurs only here and should be emended to שַׁחַר (shakhar, “dawn”) with the mem (מ) being understood as dittographic (note the final mem [ם] on the preceding word). The phrase “womb [i.e., “birth”] of dawn” refers to sunrise.
sn The point of the metaphor is not entirely clear. The dew may symbolize the king’s youthful vitality or, more likely (note the parallelism), may refer to his army of strong, youthful warriors.
tn Heb “to you [is].”

4.note: sn You are an eternal priest. The Davidic king exercised a non-Levitical priestly role. The king superintended Judah’s cultic ritual, had authority over the Levites, and sometimes led in formal worship. David himself instructed the Levites to bring the ark of the covenant to Jerusalem (1 Chr 15:11-15), joined the procession, offered sacrifices, wore a priestly ephod, and blessed the people (2 Sam 6:12-19). At the dedication of the temple Solomon led the ceremony, offering sacrifices and praying on behalf of the people (1 Kgs 8).

tn The phrase עַל־דִּבְרָתִי (’al-divratiy) is a variant of עַל־דִּבְרָת (’al-divrat; the final yod [י] being an archaic genitival ending), which in turn is a variant of עַל דָּבַר (’al davar). Both phrases can mean “concerning” or “because of,” but neither of these nuances fits the use of עַל־דִּבְרָתִי in Ps 110:4. Here the phrase probably carries the sense “according to the manner of.” See L. C. Allen, Psalms 101-150 (WBC), 81.

sn The Davidic king’s priestly role is analogous to that of Melchizedek, who was both “king of Salem” (i.e., Jerusalem) and a “priest of God Most High” in the time of Abraham (Gen 14:18-20). Like Melchizedek, the Davidic king was a royal priest, distinct from the Aaronic line (see Heb 7). The analogy focuses on the king’s priestly role; the language need not imply that Melchizedek himself was “an eternal priest.”

5.anchor: he strikes down
5.note: tn The perfect verbal forms in vv. 5-6 are understood here as descriptive-dramatic or as generalizing. Another option is to take them as rhetorical. In this case the psalmist describes anticipated events as if they had already taken place.

5.anchor: kings in the day he unleashes his anger.
5.note: tn Heb “in the day of his anger.”

6.anchor: he shatters their heads over the vast battlefield.
6.note: tn Heb “he strikes [the verb is מָחַץ (makhats), translated “strikes down” in v. 5] head[s] over a great land.” The Hebrew term רַבָּה (rabbah, “great”) is here used of distance or spatial measurement (see 1 Sam 26:13).

7.anchor: then he lifts up his head.
7.note: tn Here the expression “lifts up the head” refers to the renewed physical strength and emotional vigor (see Ps 3:3) provided by the refreshing water. For another example of a victorious warrior being energized by water in the aftermath of battle, see Judg 15:18-19 (see also 1 Sam 30:11-12, where the setting is different, however).

1.anchor: O God whom I praise, do not ignore me!
1.note: tn Heb “do not be deaf.”

2.note: tn Heb “for a mouth of evil and a mouth of deceit against me they open, they speak with me [with] a tongue of falsehood.”

3.anchor: They surround me and say hateful things;
3.note: tn Heb “and [with] words of hatred they surround me.”
