4.note: tn Heb “and I, prayer.”

5.anchor: They repay me evil for good,
5.note: tn Heb “and they set upon me evil in place of good.”

6.note: tn The prefixed verbal form is taken as a jussive here (note the imperative in the preceding line).

7.anchor: When he is judged, he will be found
7.note: tn The prefixed verbal form could be taken as a jussive, but the use of the imperfect form in the following line suggests that v. 7 anticipates the outcome of the accusation envisioned in v. 6.
tn Heb “he will go out [as] a criminal” (that is, guilty).

8.note: tn The Hebrew noun פְּקֻדָּה (pÿquddah) can mean “charge” or “office,” though BDB 824 s.v. suggests that here it refers to his possessions.

9.note: tn Or “sons.”

10.anchor: asking for handouts as they leave their ruined home!
10.note: tn Heb “and roaming, may his children roam and beg, and seek from their ruins.” Some, following the LXX, emend the term וְדָרְשׁוּ (vÿdoreshu, “and seek”) to יְגֹרְשׁוּ (yÿgoreshu; a Pual jussive, “may they be driven away” [see Job 30:5; cf. NIV, NRSV]), but דָּרַשׁ (darash) nicely parallels שִׁאֵלוּ (shi’elu, “and beg”) in the preceding line.

11.anchor: May strangers loot his property!
11.note: tn Heb “the product of his labor.”

12.anchor: May no one have compassion
12.note: tn Perhaps this refers to being generous (see Ps 37:21).

13.anchor: May the memory of them be wiped out by the time the next generation arrives!
13.note: tn Heb “in another generation may their name be wiped out.”

14.anchor: May his mother’s sin not be forgotten!
14.note: tn Heb “not be wiped out.”sn According to ancient Israelite theology and its doctrine of corporate solidarity and responsibility, children could be and often were punished for the sins of their parents. For a discussion of this issue see J. Kaminsky, Corporate Responsibility in the Hebrew Bible (JSOTSup). (Kaminsky, however, does not deal with Ps 109.)

15.anchor: and cut off the memory of his children
15.note: tn Heb “their memory.” The plural pronominal suffix probably refers back to the children mentioned in v. 13, and for clarity this has been specified in the translation.

16.anchor: and killed the disheartened.
16.note: tn Heb “and he chased an oppressed and needy man, and one timid of heart to put [him] to death.”

17.anchor: He had no desire to bless anyone, so he has experienced no blessings.
17.note: tn Heb “and he did not delight in a blessing and it is far from him.”

18.anchor: and seeped into his bones like oil.
18.note: tn Heb “and it came like water into his inner being, and like oil into his bones.” This may refer to this individual’s appetite for cursing. For him cursing was as refreshing as drinking water or massaging oneself with oil. Another option is that the destructive effects of a curse are in view. In this case a destructive curse invades his very being, like water or oil. Some who interpret the verse this way prefer to repoint the vav (ו) on “it came” to a conjunctive vav and interpret the prefixed verb as a jussive, “may it come!”

19.note: tn The Hebrew noun מֵזַח (mezakh, “belt; waistband”) occurs only here in the OT. The form apparently occurs in Isa 23:10 as well, but an emendation is necessary there.

20.anchor: those who say evil things about
20.note: tn Or “against.”
tn The Hebrew term נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh, “being; soul”) with a pronominal suffix is often equivalent to a pronoun, especially in poetry (see BDB 660 s.v. נֶפֶשׁ 4.a).

21.anchor: intervene on my behalf for the sake of your reputation!
21.note: tn Heb “but you, Lord, Master, do with me for the sake of your name.” Here “name” stands metonymically for God’s reputation.

22.anchor: and my heart beats violently within me.
22.note: tc The verb in the Hebrew text (חָלַל, khalal) appears to be a Qal form from the root חלל meaning “pierced; wounded.” However, the Qal of this root is otherwise unattested. The translation assumes an emendation to יָחִיל (yakhil), a Qal imperfect from חוּל (khul, “tremble”) or to חֹלַל (kholal), a polal perfect from חוּל (khul). See Ps 55:4, which reads לִבִּי יָחִיל בְּקִרְבִּי (libbiy yakhil bÿqirbbiy, “my heart trembles [i.e., “beats violently”] within me”).

23.anchor: I am fading away like a shadow at the end of the day;
23.note: tn Heb “like a shadow when it is extended I go.” He is like a late afternoon shadow made by the descending sun that will soon be swallowed up by complete darkness. See Ps 102:11.

24.anchor: I have turned into skin and bones.
24.note: tn Heb “and my flesh is lean away from fatness [i.e., “lean so as not to be fat”].”

25.anchor: When they see me, they shake their heads.
25.note: sn They shake their heads. Apparently shaking the head was a taunting gesture. See also Job 16:4; Ps 22:7; Lam 2:15.

26.anchor: Because you are faithful to me, deliver me!
26.note: tn Heb “deliver me according to your faithfulness.”

27.anchor: Then they will realize
27.note: tn After the preceding imperative, the prefixed verbal form with vav (ו) conjunctive indicates purpose or result.

27.anchor: this is your work,
27.note: tn Heb “that your hand [is] this.”

28.anchor: When they attack, they will be humiliated,
28.note: tn The verbal sequence is perfect + prefixed form with vav (ו) consecutive. Since the psalmist seems to be anticipating the demise of his enemies, he may be using these forms rhetorically to describe the enemies’ defeat as if it were already accomplished. Some emend the text to קָמוּ יֵבֹשׁוּ (qamu yevoshu, “may those who attack me be humiliated”). See L. C. Allen, Psalms 101-150 (WBC), 75.

29.note: tn Heb “clothed.” Another option is to translate the prefixed verbal forms in this line and the next as jussives (“may my accusers be covered with shame”).

30.anchor: in the middle of a crowd

31.anchor: to deliver him from those who threaten

2.note: tn Heb “sought out.”

3.note: tn Or “stands.”

4.note: tn Or “did,” if this refers primarily to the events of the exodus and conquest period (see vv. 6, 9).

4.anchor: amazing things that will be remembered;
4.note: tn Heb “a memorial he had made for his amazing deeds.”

5.anchor: he always remembers his covenant.
5.note: tn Or “he remembers his covenant forever” (see Ps 105:8).

6.anchor: giving them a land that belonged to other nations.
6.note: tn Heb “the strength of his deeds he proclaimed to his people, to give to them an inheritance of nations.”

7.note: tn That is, fair and for man’s good.

8.anchor: and should be faithfully and properly carried out.
8.note: tn Heb “done in faithfulness and uprightness.” The passive participle probably has the force of a gerund. See L. C. Allen, Psalms 101-150 (WBC), 89.

9.anchor: he ordained that his covenant be observed forever.
9.note: tn Heb “he commanded forever his covenant.”

10.note: tn Heb “his praise stands forever.”

1.anchor: who takes great delight in keeping his commands.
1.note: tn Heb “in his commands he delights very much.” The words “in keeping” are supplied in the translation for clarification. Taking delight in the law is metonymic here for obeying God’s moral will. See Ps 1:2.

2.note: tn Heb “His seed will be mighty on the earth, the generation of the godly.” The Hebrew term דוֹר (dor, “generation”) could be taken as parallel to “offspring” and translated “posterity,” but the singular more likely refers to the godly as a class. See BDB 189-90 s.v. for other examples where “generation” refers to a class of people.

3.note: tn Heb “stands forever.”

4.anchor: for each one who is merciful, compassionate, and just.
4.note: tn Heb “merciful and compassionate and just.” The Hebrew text has three singular adjectives, which are probably substantival and in apposition to the “godly” (which is plural, however). By switching to the singular, the psalmist focuses on each individual member of the group known as the “godly.” Note how vv. 5-9, like vv. 1-2a, use the singular to describe the representative godly individual who typifies the whole group.

5.anchor: and conducts his business honestly.
5.note: tn Heb “he sustains his matters with justice.”

6.anchor: others will always remember one who is just.
6.note: tn Heb “for an eternal memorial a just [one] will be.”

7.note: tn Heb “his heart,” viewed here as the seat of the volition and emotions (see Ps 108:1).
tn The passive participle בָּטֻחַ [בָּטוּחַ] (batuakh [batuakh]) expresses a state that results from the subject’s action. See Isa 26:3.

8.note: tn Heb “his heart,” viewed here as the seat of the volition.

9.anchor: He will be vindicated and honored.
9.note: tn Heb “his horn will be lifted up in honor.” The horn of an ox underlies the metaphor (see Deut 33:17; 1 Kgs 22:11; Ps 92:10). The horn of the wild ox is frequently a metaphor for military strength; the idiom “exalt/lift up the horn” signifies military victory (see 1 Sam 2:10; Pss 89:17, 24; 92:10; Lam 2:17).

10.anchor: the desire of the wicked will perish.
10.note: tn This could mean that the desires of the wicked will go unfulfilled. Another possibility is that “desire” refers by metonymy to the object desired and acquired. In this case the point is that the wicked will lose what they desired so badly and acquired by evil means (see Ps 10:3).

1.note: tn Heb “very great.”

3.anchor: and travels along on the wings of the wind.
3.note: sn Verse 3 may depict the Lord riding a cherub, which is in turn propelled by the wind current. Another option is that the wind is personified as a cherub. See Ps 18:10 and the discussion of ancient Near Eastern parallels to the imagery in M. Weinfeld, “‘Rider of the Clouds’ and ‘Gatherer of the Clouds’,” JANESCU 5 (1973): 422-24.

4.anchor: and the flaming fire his attendant.
4.note: tc Heb “and his attendants a flaming fire.” The lack of agreement between the singular “fire” and plural “attendants” has prompted various emendations. Some read “fire and flame.” The present translation assumes an emendation to “his attendant” (יו in the Hebrew text being virtually dittographic).sn In Ugaritic mythology Yam’s messengers appear as flaming fire before the assembly of the gods. See G. R. Driver, Canaanite Myths and Legends, 42.

6.note: tn Heb “stood.”
sn Verse 6 refers to the condition described in Gen 1:2 (note the use of the Hebrew term תְּהוֹם [tÿhom, “watery deep”] in both texts).

8.anchor: to the place you appointed for them.
8.note: tn Heb “from your shout they fled, from the sound of your thunder they hurried off.”sn Verses 7-8 poetically depict Gen 1:9-10.

9.anchor: so that they would not cover the earth again.
9.note: tn Heb “a boundary you set up, they will not cross, they will not return to cover the earth.”

10.note: tn Heb “[the] one who sends springs into streams.” Another option is to translate, “he sends streams [i.e., streams that originate from springs] into the valleys” (cf. NIV).

12.anchor: they chirp among the bushes.
12.note: tn Heb “among the thick foliage they give a sound.”

13.anchor: the earth is full of the fruit you cause to grow.
13.note: tn Heb “from the fruit of your works the earth is full.” The translation assumes that “fruit” is literal here. If “fruit” is understood more abstractly as “product; result,” then one could translate, “the earth flourishes as a result of your deeds” (cf. NIV, NRSV, REB).

14.anchor: so they can produce food from the ground,
14.note: tn Heb “to cause food to come out from the earth.”

15.anchor: as well as food that sustains people’s lives.
15.note: tn Heb “and food [that] sustains the heart of man.”

16.anchor: Lord
16.note: sn The trees of the Lord are the cedars of Lebanon (see the next line), which are viewed as special because of their great size and grandeur. The Lebanon forest was viewed elsewhere in the OT as the “garden of God” (see Ezek 31:8).

16.anchor: receive all the rain they need,
16.note: tn Heb “are satisfied,” which means here that they receive abundant rain (see v. 13).

17.anchor: near the evergreens in which the herons live.
17.note: tn Heb “[the] heron [in the] evergreens [is] its home.”sn The cedars and evergreens of the Lebanon forest are frequently associated (see, for example, 2 Chr 2:8; Isa 14:8; 37:24; Ezek 31:8).

18.anchor: The wild goats live in the high mountains;
18.note: tn Heb “the high mountains [are] for the goats.”

19.anchor: and the sun sets according to a regular schedule.
19.note: tn Heb more metaphorically, “knows its setting.”

20.anchor: You make it dark and night comes,
20.note: tn Heb “you make darkness, so that it might be night.”

21.anchor: seeking their food from God.
21.note: sn The lions’ roaring is viewed as a request for food from God.

22.note: tn Heb “lie down.”

23.anchor: and labor away until evening.
23.note: tn Heb “man goes out to his work, and to his labor until evening.”

24.anchor: You have exhibited great skill in making all of them;
24.note: tn Heb “all of them with wisdom you have made.”

25.anchor: which teems with innumerable swimming creatures,
25.note: tn Heb “where [there are] swimming things, and without number.”

26.anchor: and over here swims the whale
26.note: tn Heb “[and] this Leviathan, [which] you formed to play in it.” Elsewhere Leviathan is a multiheaded sea monster that symbolizes forces hostile to God (see Ps 74:14; Isa 27:1), but here it appears to be an actual marine creature created by God, probably some type of whale.

27.anchor: to provide them with food on a regular basis.
27.note: tn Heb “to give their food in its time.”

28.anchor: you open your hand and they are filled with food.

29.anchor: When you ignore them, they panic.
29.note: tn Heb “you hide your face, they are terrified.”

31.anchor: find pleasure in the living things he has made!

33.anchor: I will sing praise to my God as long as I exist!

34.note: tn That is, the psalmist’s thoughts as expressed in his songs of praise.

3.note: tn Heb “from the rising of the sun to its setting.” The extent is not temporal (“from sunrise to sunset”) but spatial (“from the place where the sun rises [the east] to the place where it sets [the west].” In the phenomenological language of OT cosmology, the sun was described as rising in the east and setting in the west.

4.anchor: his splendor reaches beyond the sky.
4.note: tn Heb “above the sky [is] his splendor.”

5.anchor: who sits on a high throne?
5.note: tn Heb “the one who makes high to sit.”

6.note: tn Heb “the one who makes low to see.”

7.anchor: and lifts up the needy from the garbage pile,
7.note: sn The language of v. 7 is almost identical to that of 1 Sam 2:8.

9.note: tn Heb “sons.”

1.anchor: for the sake of your loyal love and faithfulness.
1.note: sn The psalmist asks the Lord to demonstrate his loyal love and faithfulness, not simply so Israel may benefit, but primarily so that the Lord will receive honor among the nations, who will recognize, contrary to their present view (see v. 2), that Israel’s God is committed to his people.

3.note: sn He does whatever he pleases. Such sovereignty is characteristic of kings (see Eccl 8:3).

4.note: tn Heb “the work of the hands of man.”

7.anchor: They cannot even clear their throats.
7.note: tn Heb “they cannot mutter in their throats.” Verse 5a refers to speaking, v. 7c to inarticulate sounds made in the throat (see M. Dahood, Psalms [AB], 3:140-41).

8.anchor: Those who make them will end up
8.note: tn Heb “will be.” Another option is to take the prefixed verbal form as a prayer, “may those who make them end up like them.” sn Because the idols are lifeless, they cannot help their worshipers in times of crisis. Consequently the worshipers end up as dead as the gods in which they trust.

9.note: tn Or “[source of] help.”
tn Heb “and their shield.”

10.note: tn Or “[source of] help.”
tn Heb “and their shield.”

11.anchor: He is their deliverer
11.note: tn Or “[source of] help.”

11.anchor: and protector.
11.note: tn Heb “and their shield.”

12.anchor: he will bless the family
12.note: tn Heb “house.”

13.note: tn Heb “the small along with the great.” The translation assumes that “small” and “great” here refer to age (see 2 Chr 15:13). Another option is to translate “both the insignificant and the prominent” (see Job 3:19; cf. NEB “high and low alike”).

14.note: tn Heb “may he add to you, to you and your sons.” The prefixed verbal form is jussive, indicating this is a prayer.

15.anchor: the creator
15.note: tn Or “maker.”

16.anchor: but the earth he has given to mankind.
16.note: tn Heb “to the sons of man.”

17.anchor: nor do any of those who descend into the silence of death.
17.note: tn Heb “silence,” a metonymy here for death (see Ps 94:17).

1.anchor: because he heard my plea for mercy,
1.note: tn Heb “I love because the Lord heard my voice, my pleas.” It is possible that “the Lord” originally appeared directly after “I love” and was later accidentally misplaced. The translation assumes the prefixed verbal form is a preterite. The psalmist recalls that God heard his cry for help (note the perfect in v. 2a and the narrative in vv. 3-4).

2.anchor: As long as I live, I will call to him when I need help.
2.note: tn Heb “and in my days I will cry out.”

3.note: tn The translation assumes the prefixed verbal form is a preterite. The psalmist recalls the crisis from which the Lord delivered him.

6.note: tn Heb “I was low.”

7.note: tn The Hebrew idiom גָּמַל עַל (gamal ’al) means “to repay,” here in a positive sense (cf. Ps 13:5).

8.note: tn Or “for.”
tn “Lord” is supplied here in the translation for clarification.

9.note: tn Heb “lands, regions.”

11.anchor: I rashly declared,
11.note: tn Heb “I said in my haste.”

13.note: tn Heb “a cup of deliverance I will lift up.” Perhaps this alludes to a drink offering the psalmist will present as he thanks the Lord for his deliverance. See v. 17.

15.anchor: the lives of his faithful followers.
15.note: tn Heb “precious in the eyes of the Lord [is] the death of his godly ones.” The point is not that God delights in or finds satisfaction in the death of his followers! The psalmist, who has been delivered from death, affirms that the life-threatening experiences of God’s followers get God’s attention, just as a precious or rare object would attract someone’s eye. See Ps 72:14 for a similar expression of this belief.

16.anchor: You saved me from death.
16.note: tn Heb “you have loosed my bonds.” In this context the imagery refers to deliverance from death (see v. 3).

1.note: tn Or “is forever.”

3.note: tn Heb “house.”

4.note: tn Heb “fearers of the Lord.” See Ps 15:4.

5.anchor: answered me and put me in a wide open place.
5.note: tn Heb “the Lord answered me in a wide open place.”

6.anchor: What can people do to me?
6.note: tn The rhetorical question assumes the answer, “Nothing!” The imperfect is used in a modal sense here, indicating capability or potential. See Ps 56:11.

7.note: tn Heb “for me.”
tn Heb “among my helpers.” The preposition may indicate identity here, while the plural may be one of majesty or respect.

8.anchor: It is better to take shelter
8.note: tn “Taking shelter” in the Lord is an idiom for seeking his protection. Seeking his protection presupposes and even demonstrates the subject’s loyalty to the Lord. In the psalms those who “take shelter” in the Lord are contrasted with the wicked and equated with those who love, fear, and serve the Lord (Pss 5:11-12; 31:17-20; 34:21-22).

10.note: tn In this context the phrase “in the name of the Lord” means “by the Lord’s power.”

tn Traditionally the verb has been derived from מוּל (mul, “to circumcise”) and translated “[I] cut [them] off” (see BDB 557-58 s.v. II מוּל). However, it is likely that this is a homonym meaning “to fend off” (see HALOT 556 s.v. II מול) or “to push away.” In this context, where the psalmist is reporting his past experience, the prefixed verbal form is best understood as a preterite. The phrase also occurs in vv. 11, 12.

12.anchor: But they disappeared as quickly
12.note: tn Heb “were extinguished.”

12.anchor: as a fire among thorns.
12.note: tn The point seems to be that the hostility of the nations (v. 10) is short-lived, like a fire that quickly devours thorns and then burns out. Some, attempting to create a better parallel with the preceding line, emend דֹּעֲכוּ (do’akhu, “they were extinguished”) to בָּעֲרוּ (ba’aru, “they burned”). In this case the statement emphasizes their hostility.

13.note: tn Heb “pushing, you pushed me.” The infinitive absolute emphasizes the following verbal idea. The psalmist appears to address the nations as if they were an individual enemy. Some find this problematic and emend the verb form (which is a Qal perfect second masculine singular with a first person singular suffix) to נִדְחֵיתִי (nidkheti), a Niphal perfect first common singular, “I was pushed.”

13.anchor: and tried to knock me down,
13.note: tn Heb “to fall,” i.e., “that [I] might fall.”

14.note: tn Or “salvation.”

15.anchor: ’s right hand conquers,
15.note: tn Heb “does valiantly.” The statement refers here to military success (see Num 24:18; 1 Sam 14:48; Pss 60:12; 108:13).

16.anchor: ’s right hand gives victory,
16.note: tn Heb “exalts.”

17.anchor: has done.
17.note: tn Heb “the works of the Lord.”

18.note: tn The infinitive absolute emphasizes the following verbal idea.

19.anchor: Open for me the gates of the just king’s temple!
19.note: tn Heb “the gates of justice.” The gates of the Lord’s temple are referred to here, as v. 20 makes clear. They are called “gates of justice” because they are the entrance to the just king’s palace. This has been specified in the translation for clarity.

22.note: tn Heb “the head of the corner.”sn The metaphor of the stone…the builders discarded describes the way in which God’s deliverance reversed the psalmist’s circumstances. When he was in distress, he was like a stone which was discarded by builders as useless, but now that he has been vindicated by God, all can see that he is of special importance to God, like the cornerstone of the building.

23.anchor: We consider it amazing!
23.note: tn Heb “it is amazing in our eyes.” The use of the plural pronoun here and in vv. 24-27 suggests that the psalmist may be speaking for the entire nation. However, it is more likely that vv. 22-27 are the people’s response to the psalmist’s thanksgiving song (see especially v. 26). They rejoice with him because his deliverance on the battlefield (see vv. 10-12) had national repercussions.

24.anchor: has brought about.
24.note: tn Heb “this is the day the Lord has made.” Though sometimes applied in a general way, this statement in its context refers to the day of deliverance which the psalmist and people celebrate.

25.note: sn A petition for deliverance and success seems odd in a psalm thanking God for deliverance, but it is not unique (see Ps 9:19-20). The people ask God to continue to intervene for them as he has for the psalmist.

26.anchor: We will pronounce blessings on you
26.note: tn The pronominal suffix is second masculine plural, but the final mem (ם) is probably dittographic (note the mem [מ] at the beginning of the following form) or enclitic, in which case the suffix may be taken as second masculine singular, referring to the psalmist.

26.anchor: ’s temple.
26.note: tn Heb “from the house of the Lord.”

27.anchor: to the horns of the altar!
27.note: tn The second half of v. 27 has been translated and interpreted in a variety of ways. For a survey of major views, see L. C. Allen, Psalms 101-150 (WBC), 122.

28.note: sn You are my God. The psalmist speaks again (see v. 21), responding to the words of the worshipers (vv. 22-27).

1.note: tn The Hebrew term מֵאַיִן (me’ayin) is interrogative, not relative, in function. Rather than directly stating that his source of help descends from the hills, the psalmist is asking, “From where does my help come?” Nevertheless, the first line does indicate that he is looking toward the hills for help, probably indicating that he is looking up toward the sky in anticipation of supernatural intervention. The psalmist assumes the dramatic role of one needing help. He answers his own question in v. 2.

2.note: tn Or “Maker.”

3.note: tn Heb “the one who guards you.”

tn The prefixed verbal forms following the negative particle אל appear to be jussives. As noted above, if they are taken as true jussives of prayer, then the speaker in v. 3 would appear to be distinct from both the speaker in vv. 1-2 and the speaker in vv. 4-8. However, according to GKC 322 §109.e), the jussives are used rhetorically here “to express the conviction that something cannot or should not happen.” In this case one should probably translate, “he will not allow your foot to slip, your protector will not sleep,” and understand just one speaker in vv. 4-8.

4.note: tn Heb “the one who guards Israel.”

6.note: sn One hardly thinks of the moon’s rays as being physically harmful, like those of the sun. The reference to the moon may simply lend poetic balance to the verse, but it is likely that the verse reflects an ancient, primitive belief that the moon could have an adverse effect on the mind (note the English expression “moonstruck,” which reflects such a belief). Another possibility is that the sun and moon stand by metonymy for harmful forces characteristic of the day and night, respectively.

8.anchor: will protect you in all you do,
8.note: tn Heb “your going out and your coming in.”

2.anchor: and those who deceive with their tongue.
2.note: tn Heb “from a tongue of deception.”

3.note: tn Heb “What will he give to you, and what will he add to you, O tongue of deception?” The psalmist addresses his deceptive enemies. The Lord is the understood subject of the verbs “give” and “add.” The second part of the question echoes a standard curse formula, “thus the Lord/God will do … and thus he will add” (see Ruth 1:17; 1 Sam 3:17; 14:44; 20:13; 25:22; 2 Sam 3:9, 35; 19:13; 1 Kgs 2:23; 2 Kgs 6:31).

4.anchor: with arrowheads forged over the hot coals.
4.note: tn Heb “with coals of the wood of the broom plant.” The wood of the broom plant was used to make charcoal, which in turn was used to fuel the fire used to forge the arrowheads.

5.anchor: I have resided among the tents of Kedar.
5.note: sn Meshech was located in central Anatolia (modern Turkey). Kedar was located in the desert to east-southeast of Israel. Because of the reference to Kedar, it is possible that Ps 120:5 refers to a different Meshech, perhaps one associated with the individual mentioned as a descendant of Aram in 1 Chr 1:17. (However, the LXX in 1 Chr 1:17 follows the parallel text in Gen 10:23, which reads “Mash,” not Meshech.) It is, of course, impossible that the psalmist could have been living in both the far north and the east at the same time. For this reason one must assume that he is recalling his experience as a wanderer among the nations or that he is using the geographical terms metaphorically and sarcastically to suggest that the enemies who surround him are like the barbarians who live in these distant regions. For a discussion of the problem, see L. C. Allen, Psalms 101-150 (WBC), 146.

6.note: tn The singular participial form probably has a representative function here. The psalmist envisions the typical hater of peace who represents the entire category of such individuals.

7.anchor: but when I speak, they want to make war.
7.note: tn Heb “they [are] for war.”

1.note: tn Heb “in the ones saying to me.” After the verb שָׂמַח (samakh), the preposition בְּ (bet) usually introduces the reason for joy.

2.note: tn Or “were.”

3.note: tc Heb “Jerusalem, which is built like a city which is joined to her together.” The meaning of the Hebrew text is unclear. Many regard this as a description of the compact way in which the city was designed or constructed. The translation assumes an emendation of the verb חֻבְּרָה (khubbÿrah, “is joined”) to a noun חֶבְרָה (khevrah, “association; company”). The text then reads literally, “Jerusalem, which is built like a city which has a company together.” This in turn can be taken as a reference to Jerusalem’s role as a city where people congregated for religious festivals and other civic occasions (see vv. 4-5).

4.note: tn Heb “[it is] a statute for Israel to give thanks to the name of the Lord.”

5.anchor: on the thrones of the house of David.
5.note: tn Heb “Indeed, there they sit [on] thrones for judgment, [on] thrones [belonging] to the house of David.”

6.anchor: May those who love her prosper!
6.note: tn Or “be secure.”

7.note: tn or “security.”
tn The psalmist uses second feminine singular pronominal forms to address personified Jerusalem.

9.anchor: I will pray for you to prosper.
9.note: tn Heb “I will seek good for you.” The psalmist will seek Jerusalem’s “good” through prayer.

1.note: tn Heb “sitting.” The Hebrew verb יָשַׁב (yashav) is here used metonymically of “sitting enthroned” (see Pss 9:7; 29:10; 55:19; 102:12).

2.anchor: as the eyes of a female servant look to the hand of her mistress,
2.note: sn Servants look to their master for food, shelter, and other basic needs.

3.anchor: For we have had our fill of humiliation, and then some.
3.note: tn Heb “for greatly we are filled [with] humiliation.”

4.note: tn Heb “greatly our soul is full to it.”

2.note: map For location see Map5-B1; Map6-F3; Map7-E2; Map8-F2; Map10-B3; JP1-F4; JP2-F4; JP3-F4; JP4-F4.

3.note: tn Heb “so that the godly might not stretch out their hands in wrongdoing.” A wicked king who sets a sinful example can have an adverse moral and ethical effect on the people he rules.

4.note: tn Heb “pure of heart.” The “heart” is here viewed as the seat of one’s moral character and motives. The “pure of heart” are God’s faithful followers who trust in and love the Lord and, as a result, experience his deliverance (see Pss 7:10; 11:2; 32:11; 36:10; 64:10; 94:15; 97:11).

5.anchor: May Israel experience peace!
5.note: tn Heb “peace [be] upon Israel.” The statement is understood as a prayer (see Ps 122:8 for a similar prayer for peace).

2.note: tn Heb “rose up against us.”

4.note: tn Or “stream.”
tn Heb “would have passed over.”
tn Heb “our being.” The Hebrew term נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh) with a pronominal suffix is often equivalent to a pronoun, especially in poetry (see BDB 660 s.v. נֶפֶשׁ 4.a).

5.anchor: would have overwhelmed us.
5.note: tn Heb “then they would have passed over our being, the raging waters.”

6.note: tn Heb “[the one] who.”

7.note: tn Heb “our life escaped.”

8.note: tn Or “Maker.”

1.note: tn Heb “walk in.”

3.note: tn Heb “walk in his ways.”

4.note: tn Heb “you, you commanded your precepts, to keep, very much.”

5.anchor: If only I were predisposed
5.note: tn Heb “if only my ways were established.”

6.note: tn Or “when.”
tn Heb “I gaze at.”

7.anchor: I will give you sincere thanks,
7.note: tn Heb “I will give you thanks with an upright heart.”

8.note: tn Heb “do not abandon me to excess.” For other uses of the phrase עַד מְאֹד (’ad mÿ’od, “to excess”), see Ps 38:6, 8.

9.anchor: By guarding it according to your instructions!
9.note: tn Heb “by keeping according to your word.” Many medieval Hebrew mss as well as the LXX read the plural, “your words.”

11.anchor: In my heart I store up
11.note: tn Or “hide.”

11.anchor: your words,
11.note: tn Heb “your word.” Some medieval Hebrew mss as well as the LXX read the plural, “your words.”

12.anchor: You deserve praise,
12.note: tn Heb “[are] blessed.”

13.anchor: all the regulations you have revealed.
13.note: tn Heb “of your mouth.”

14.note: tn Heb “as upon,” meaning “as if” (see 2 Chr 32:19).

14.anchor: they were riches of all kinds.
14.note: tn Heb “all wealth.” The phrase refers to all kinds of wealth and riches. See Prov 1:13; 6:31; 24:4; Ezek 27:12, 18.

15.anchor: and focus
15.note: tn Heb “gaze [at].”

15.anchor: on your behavior.
15.note: tn Heb “ways” (referring figuratively to God’s behavior here).

16.anchor: I do not forget your instructions.
16.note: tn Heb “your word.” Many medieval Hebrew mss as well as the LXX read the plural here.

17.anchor: Then I will live
17.note: tn The prefixed verbal form is probably a cohortative indicating purpose/result after the preceding imperative.

17.anchor: and keep
17.note: tn The cohortative with vav (ו) conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the imperative that begins the verse.

17.anchor: your instructions.
17.note: tn Heb “your word.” Many medieval Hebrew mss as well as several ancient versions read the plural here.

18.note: tn Heb “uncover.” The verb form גַּל (gal) is an apocopated Piel imperative from גָּלָה (galah, see GKC 214 §75.cc).

18.anchor: my eyes so I can truly see
18.note: tn The cohortative with vav (ו) conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the preceding imperative.

19.anchor: I am like a foreigner in this land.
19.note: tn Heb “I am a resident alien in the land.” Resident aliens were especially vulnerable and in need of help. They needed to know the social and legal customs of the land to avoid getting into trouble. The translation (note the addition of “like”) assumes the psalmist is speaking metaphorically, not literally.

20.note: tn Heb “my soul languishes for longing for.”

21.anchor: Those who stray from your commands are doomed.
21.note: tn Heb “accursed.” The traditional punctuation of the Hebrew text takes “accursed” with the previous line (“arrogant, accursed ones”), but it is preferable to take it with the second line as the predicate of the statement.

22.note: tn Heb “roll away from upon me.” Some derive the imperatival form גַּל (gal) from גָּלָה (galah, “uncover,” as in v. 18), but here the form is from גָּלַל (galal, “roll”; see Josh 5:9, where חֶרְפָּה [kherpah, “shame; reproach”] also appears as object of the verb). Some, following the lead of a Dead Sea scroll (11QPsa), emend the form to גֹּל (gol).

23.anchor: Though rulers plot and slander me,
23.note: tn Heb “though rulers sit, about me they talk together.” (For another example of the Niphal of דָּבַר (davar) used with a suffixed form of the preposition ב, see Ezek 33:30.)

24.anchor: they give me guidance.
24.note: tn Heb “men of my counsel.” That is, God’s rules are like advisers to the psalmist, for they teach him how to live in a godly manner that refutes the accusations of his enemies.

25.note: tn Heb “according to your word.” Many medieval Hebrew mss read the plural “your words.”

26.anchor: I told you about my ways
26.note: tn Heb “my ways I proclaimed.”

27.anchor: Then I can meditate
27.note: tn The cohortative with vav (ו) conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the preceding imperative.

27.anchor: on your marvelous teachings.
27.note: tn Heb “your amazing things,” which refers here to the teachings of the law (see v. 18).

28.note: tn Heb “according to your word.” Many medieval Hebrew mss read the plural “your words.”

29.note: tn Heb “be gracious to me.” The verb is used metonymically here for “graciously giving” the law. (See Gen 33:5, where Jacob uses this verb in describing how God had graciously given him children.)

30.note: tn BDB 1000-1001 s.v. I שָׁוָה derives the verb from the first homonym listed, meaning “to agree with; to be like; to resemble.” It here means (in the Piel stem) “to be accounted suitable,” which in turn would mean by metonymy “to accept; to be committed to.” Some prefer to derive the verb from a homonym meaning “to place; to set,” but in this case an elliptical prepositional phrase must be understood, “I place your regulations [before me]” (see Ps 16:8).

32.anchor: for you enable me to do so.
32.note: tn Heb “for you make wide my heart.” The “heart” is viewed here as the seat of the psalmist’s volition and understanding. The Lord gives the psalmist the desire and moral understanding that are foundational to the willing obedience depicted metaphorically in the preceding line. In Isa 60:5 the expression “your heart will be wide” means “your heart will swell with pride,” but here the nuance appears to be different.

33.anchor: so that I might observe it continually.
33.note: tn Heb “and I will keep it to the end.” The prefixed verbal form with vav (ו) conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the preceding imperative. The Hebrew term עֵקֶב (’eqev) is understood to mean “end” here. Another option is to take עֵקֶב (’eqev) as meaning “reward” here (see Ps 19:11) and to translate, “so that I might observe it and be rewarded.”

34.anchor: and keep it with all my heart.
34.note: tn The two prefixed verbal forms with vav (ו) conjunctive indicate purpose/result after the introductory imperative.

35.anchor: for I delight to walk in it.
35.note: tn Heb “for in it I delight.”

36.anchor: rather than for wealth gained unjustly.

38.anchor: which you made to the one who honors you.
38.note: tn Heb “which [is] for your fear,” that is, the promise made to those who exhibit fear of God.

42.anchor: Then I will have a reply for the one who insults me,
42.note: tn Heb “and I will answer [the] one who insults me a word.” The prefixed verbal form with vav (ו) conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the jussive (see v. 41).

43.anchor: Do not completely deprive me of a truthful testimony,
43.note: tn Heb “do not snatch from my mouth a word of truth to excess.” The psalmist wants to be able to give a reliable testimony about the Lord’s loyal love (vv. 41-42), but if God does not intervene, the psalmist will be deprived of doing so, for the evidence of such love (i.e., deliverance) will be lacking.

45.note: tn Heb “and I will walk about in a wide place.” The cohortative with prefixed vav (ו) conjunctive gives a further consequence of the anticipated positive divine response (see vv. 43-44). Another option is to take the cohortative as expressing the psalmist’s request. In this case one could translate, “and please give me security.”

46.note: tn The series of four cohortatives with prefixed vav (ו) conjunctive in vv. 46-48 list further consequences of the anticipated positive divine response to the request made in v. 43.

48.anchor: I will lift my hands to
48.note: tn Lifting the hands is often associated with prayer (Pss 28:2; 63:4; Lam 2:19). (1) Because praying to God’s law borders on the extreme, some prefer to emend the text to “I lift up my hands to you,” eliminating “your commands, which I love” as dittographic. In this view these words were accidentally repeated from the previous verse. (2) However, it is possible that the psalmist closely associates the law with God himself because he views the law as the expression of the divine will. (3) Another option is that “lifting the hands” does not refer to prayer here, but to the psalmist’s desire to receive and appropriate the law. (4) Still others understand this to be an action praising God’s commands (so NCV; cf. TEV, CEV, NLT).

50.note: tn The hope generated by the promise (see v. 49b) brings comfort because (note “for” at the beginning of the line) the promise revives the psalmist’s spirits. Another option is to take כִּי (ki) at the beginning of the second line in the sense of “that,” in which case “this” refers to the promise’s power to revive.

51.anchor: Arrogant people do nothing but scoff at me.
51.note: tn Heb “scoff at me to excess.”

54.anchor: in the house where I live.
54.note: tn Heb “in the house of my dwelling place.” Some take the Hebrew noun מָגוֹר (magor) in the sense of “temporary abode,” and see this as a reference to the psalmist’s status as a resident alien (see v. 19). But the noun can refer to a dwelling place in general (see Ps 55:15).

55.note: tn The cohortative verbal form expresses the psalmist’s resolve to obey the law.

56.note: tn Heb “this has been to me.” The demonstrative “this” (1) refers back to the practices mentioned in vv. 54-55, or (2) looks forward to the statement in the second line, in which case the כִּי (ki) at the beginning of the second line should be translated “that.”

57.note: tn Heb “to keep your words” (see v. 9).

58.anchor: Have mercy on me as you promised!

59.note: tn Heb “and I turn my feet toward.”

60.note: tn Heb “I hurry and I do not delay to keep your commands.”

62.note: tn The psalmist uses an imperfect verbal form to emphasize that this is his continuing practice.

63.anchor: I am a friend to all your loyal followers,
63.note: tn Heb “to all who fear you.”

73.note: tn The cohortative verbal form with vav (ו) conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the preceding imperative.

74.note: tn Heb “those who fear you will see me and rejoice.”
