80.note: tn Heb “may my heart be complete in your statutes.”

81.note: tn Heb “my soul pines for.” See Ps 84:2.

83.note: tn The Hebrew word נֹאד (no’d, “leather container”) refers to a container made from animal skin which is used to hold wine or milk (see Josh 9:4, 13; Judg 4:19; 1 Sam 16:20).

84.anchor: How long must your servant endure this?
84.note: tn Heb “How long are the days of your servant?”

85.note: tn Heb “which [is] not according to your law.”

86.note: sn God’s commands are a reliable guide to right and wrong. By keeping them the psalmist is doing what is right, yet he is still persecuted.

88.note: tn The cohortative verbal form with vav (ו) conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the preceding imperative.

89.note: tn Heb “Forever, O Lord, your word stands firm in heaven,” or “Forever, O Lord, [is] your word; it stands firm in heaven.” The translation assumes that “your word” refers here to the body of divine instructions contained in the law (note the frequent references to the law in vv. 92-96). See vv. 9, 16-17, 57, 101, 105, 130, 139 and 160-61. The reference in v. 86 to God’s law being faithful favors this interpretation. Another option is that “your word” refers to God’s assuring word of promise, mentioned in vv. 25, 28, 42, 65, 74, 81, 107, 114, 147 and 169. In this case one might translate, “O Lord, your promise is reliable, it stands firm in heaven.”

90.anchor: You demonstrate your faithfulness to all generations.
90.note: tn Heb “to a generation and a generation [is] your faithfulness.”

92.anchor: I would have died in my sorrow.

95.anchor: The wicked prepare to kill me,
95.note: tn Heb “the wicked wait for me to kill me.”

96.anchor: but your commands are beyond full comprehension.
96.note: tn Heb “to every perfection I have seen an end, your command is very wide.” God’s law is beyond full comprehension, which is why the psalmist continually studies it (vv. 95, 97).

98.note: tn The plural form needs to be revocalized as a singular in order to agree with the preceding singular verb and the singular pronoun in the next line. The Lord’s “command” refers here to the law (see Ps 19:8).

101.anchor: so that I might keep your instructions.
101.note: tn Heb “your word.” Many medieval Hebrew mss read the plural.

103.note: tn Heb “How smooth they are to my palate, your word, more than honey to my mouth.” A few medieval Hebrew mss, as well as several other ancient witnesses, read the plural “your words,” which can then be understood as the subject of the plural verb “they are smooth.”

104.anchor: Therefore I hate all deceitful actions.

105.anchor: and a light to illumine my path.
105.note: tn Heb “[is] a lamp for my foot and a light for my path.”

108.anchor: , please accept the freewill offerings of my praise!

109.anchor: My life is in continual danger,
109.note: tn Heb “my life [is] in my hands continually.”

111.note: tn Heb “for the joy of my heart [are] they.”

112.anchor: I hate people with divided loyalties,
112.note: tn Heb “divided ones.” The word occurs only here; it appears to be derived from a verbal root, attested in Arabic, meaning “to split” (see HALOT 762 s.v. *סֵעֵף). Since the psalmist is emphasizing his unswerving allegiance to God and his law, the term probably refers to those who lack such loyalty. The translation is similar to that suggested by L. C. Allen, Psalms 101-150 (WBC), 131.

115.note: tn The cohortative verbal form with vav (ו) conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the preceding imperative.
tn The psalmist has already declared that he observes God’s commands despite persecution, so here the idea must be “so that I might observe the commands of my God unhindered by threats.”

116.note: tn Heb “do not make me ashamed of my hope.” After the Hebrew verb בּוֹשׁ (bosh, “to be ashamed”) the preposition מִן (min, “from”) often introduces the reason for shame.

117.note: tn Or “and that I might focus.” The two cohortatives with vav (ו) conjunctive indicate purpose/result after the imperative at the beginning of the verse.

118.anchor: for they are deceptive and unreliable.

119.note: sn As he explains in the next verse, the psalmist’s fear of judgment motivates him to obey God’s rules.

120.note: tn The Hebrew verb סָמַר (samar, “to tremble”) occurs only here and in Job 4:15.
tn Heb “from fear of you.” The pronominal suffix on the noun is an objective genitive.

121.anchor: I do what is fair and right.

122.anchor: Guarantee the welfare of your servant!
122.note: tn Heb “be surety for your servant for good.”

123.anchor: for your reliable promise to be fulfilled.
123.note: tn Heb “and for the word of your faithfulness.”

124.anchor: Show your servant your loyal love!
124.note: tn Heb “do with your servant according to your loyal love.”

125.note: tn or “know.” The cohortative verbal form with vav (ו) conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the preceding imperative.

127.note: tn “For this reason” connects logically with the statement made in v. 126. Because the judgment the psalmist fears (see vv. 119-120) is imminent, he remains loyal to God’s law.

130.note: tn Or “the [morally] naive,” that is, the one who is young and still in the process of learning right from wrong and distinguishing wisdom from folly. See Pss 19:7; 116:6.

131.note: tn The verb occurs only here in the OT.

132.anchor: as you typically do to your loyal followers.
132.note: tn Heb “according to custom toward the lovers of your name.” The “lovers of” God’s “name” are the Lord’s loyal followers. See Pss 5:11; 69:36; Isa 56:6.

133.anchor: Direct my steps by your word!
133.note: tn God’s “word” refers here to his law (see v. 11).

134.note: tn The cohortative verbal form with vav (ו) conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the preceding imperative.

135.note: tn Heb “cause your face to shine.”

136.note: tn Heb “they”; even though somewhat generic, the referent (people) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

138.anchor: The rules you impose are just,
138.note: tn Heb “you commanded [in] justice your rules.”

139.anchor: for my enemies forget your instructions.

144.anchor: Give me insight so that I can live.
144.note: tn The cohortative verbal form with vav (ו) conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the preceding imperative.

146.note: tn The cohortative verbal form with vav (ו) conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the preceding imperative.

149.anchor: , revive me, as you typically do!

150.anchor: Those who are eager to do

151.anchor: and all your commands are reliable.

152.anchor: you ordained your rules to last.
152.note: tn Heb “long ago I knew concerning your rules, that forever you established them.” See v. 89 for the same idea. The translation assumes that the preposition מִן (min) prefixed to “your rules” introduces the object of the verb יָדַע (yada’), as in 1 Sam 23:23. Another option is that the preposition indicates source, in which case one might translate, “Long ago I realized from your rules that forever you established them” (cf. NIV, NRSV).

154.note: tn Heb “and redeem me.” The verb “redeem” casts the Lord in the role of a leader who protects members of his extended family in times of need and crisis (see Ps 19:14).

155.anchor: The wicked have no chance for deliverance,
155.note: tn Heb “far from the wicked [is] deliverance.”

156.anchor: Revive me, as you typically do!

157.anchor: The enemies who chase me are numerous.
157.note: tn Heb “many [are] those who chase me and my enemies.”

158.anchor: because they do not keep your instructions.

160.note: tn Heb “the head of your word is truth, and forever [is] all your just regulation.” The term “head” is used here of the “sum total” of God’s instructions.

161.anchor: yet I am more afraid of disobeying your instructions.
161.note: tn Heb “and because of your instructions my heart trembles.” The psalmist’s healthy “fear” of the consequences of violating God’s instructions motivates him to obey them. See v. 120.

162.anchor: like one who finds much plunder.
162.note: tn Heb “like one who finds great plunder.” See Judg 5:30. The image is that of a victorious warrior who finds a large amount of plunder on the field of battle.

164.note: tn The number “seven” is use rhetorically to suggest thoroughness.

165.note: tn Heb “and there is no stumbling to them.”

168.anchor: for you are aware of everything I do.
168.note: tn Heb “for all my ways [are] before you.”

169.anchor: Listen to my cry for help,
169.note: tn Heb “may my cry approach before you.”

172.anchor: May my tongue sing about your instructions,

173.note: tn The words “to obey” are not in the Hebrew text, but have been supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons and for clarity.

175.note: tn God’s regulations will “help” the psalmist by giving him moral and ethical guidance.

176.anchor: I have wandered off like a lost sheep.
176.note: tn Heb “I stray like a lost sheep.” It is possible that the point of the metaphor is vulnerability: The psalmist, who is threatened by his enemies, feels as vulnerable as a straying, lost sheep. This would not suggest, however, that he has wandered from God’s path (see the second half of the verse, as well as v. 110).

1.anchor: each one who keeps his commands!
1.note: tn Heb “the one who walks in his ways.”

2.anchor: You will be blessed and secure.
2.note: tn Heb “how blessed you [will be] and it will be good for you.”

3.note: tn One could translate “sons” (see Ps 127:3 and the note on the word “sons” there), but here the term seems to refer more generally to children of both genders.

4.anchor: will be blessed in this way.
4.note: tn Heb “look, indeed thus will the man, the fearer of the Lord, be blessed.”

5.anchor: that you might see
5.note: tn The imperative with prefixed vav (ו) conjunctive indicates purpose/result after the preceding jussive.

5.anchor: Jerusalem
5.note: map For location see Map5-B1; Map6-F3; Map7-E2; Map8-F2; Map10-B3; JP1-F4; JP2-F4; JP3-F4; JP4-F4.

6.note: tn Heb “peace [be] upon Israel.” The statement is understood as a prayer (see Ps 125:5).

1.note: sn The city symbolizes community security, which is the necessary framework for family security.

2.note: tn Here the Hebrew particle כֵּן (ken) is used to stress the following affirmation (see Josh 2:4; Ps 63:2).

2.anchor: he can provide for those whom he loves even when they sleep.
2.note: tn Heb “he gives to his beloved, sleep.” The translation assumes that the Hebrew term שֵׁנָא (shena’, “sleep,” an alternate form of שֵׁנָה, shenah) is an adverbial accusative. The point seems to be this: Hard work by itself is not what counts, but one’s relationship to God, for God is able to bless an individual even while he sleeps. (There may even be a subtle allusion to the miracle of conception following sexual intercourse; see the reference to the gift of sons in the following verse.) The statement is not advocating laziness, but utilizing hyperbole to give perspective and to remind the addressees that God must be one’s first priority. Another option is to take “sleep” as the direct object: “yes, he gives sleep to his beloved” (cf. NIV, NRSV). In this case the point is this: Hard work by itself is futile, for only God is able to bless one with sleep, which metonymically refers to having one’s needs met. He blesses on the basis of one’s relationship to him, not on the basis of physical energy expended.

3.note: tn or “look.”

tn Some prefer to translate this term with the gender neutral “children,” but “sons” are plainly in view here, as the following verses make clear. Daughters are certainly wonderful additions to a family, but in ancient Israelite culture sons were the “arrows” that gave a man security in his old age, for they could defend the family interests at the city gate, where the legal and economic issues of the community were settled.

4.anchor: are like arrows in a warrior’s hand.
4.note: tn Heb “like arrows in the hand of a warrior, so [are] sons of youth.” Arrows are used in combat to defend oneself against enemies; sons are viewed here as providing social security and protection (see v. 5). The phrase “sons of youth” is elliptical, meaning “sons [born during the father’s] youth.” Such sons will have grown up to be mature adults and will have children of their own by the time the father reaches old age and becomes vulnerable to enemies. Contrast the phrase “son of old age” in Gen 37:3 (see also 44:20), which refers to Jacob’s age when Joseph was born.

5.anchor: They will not be put to shame
5.note: tn Being “put to shame” is here metonymic for being defeated, probably in a legal context, as the reference to the city gate suggests. One could be humiliated (Ps 69:12) or deprived of justice (Amos 5:12) at the gate, but with strong sons to defend the family interests this was less likely to happen.

5.anchor: when they confront
5.note: tn Heb “speak with.”

1.note: tn Heb “we were like dreamers.” This could mean the speakers were so overcome with ecstatic joy (see v. 3b) that they were like those who fantasize about pleasurable experiences in their sleep (see Isa 29:7-8). Since dreams are more commonly associated in the OT with prophetic visions, the community may be comparing their experience of God’s renewed favor to a prophet’s receiving divine visions. Just as a prophetic dream sweeps the individual into a different dimension and sometimes brings one face-to-face with God himself (see Gen 28:11-15; 1 Kgs 3:5-15), so the community was aware of God’s presence in a special way in the day of Zion’s restoration. Though the MT as it stands makes good sense, some choose to understand a homonymic root here meaning “to be healthy; to be strong” (see BDB 321 s.v. I חָלַם) and translate, “we were like those restored to health.” This reading appears to have the support of several ancient translations as well as 11QPsa. See L. C. Allen (Psalms 101-150 [WBC], 170-71) for a discussion of the viewpoints.

2.anchor: At that time the nations said,
2.note: tn Heb “they said among the nations.”

4.anchor: just as the streams in the arid south are replenished.
4.note: tn Heb “like the streams in the Negev.”sn The streams in the arid south. Y. Aharoni writes of the streams in the Negev: “These usually dry wadis collect water on rainy days from vast areas. The situation is also aggravated by floods from the desert mountains and southern Judah. For a day or two or, more frequently, for only a few hours they turn into dangerous torrents” (Y. Aharoni, The Land of the Bible, 26). God’s people were experiencing a “dry season” after a time of past blessing; they pray here for a “flash flood” of his renewed blessing. This does not imply that they are requesting only a brief display of God’s blessing. Rather the point of comparison is the suddenness with which the wadis swell during a rain, as well as the depth and power of these raging waters. The community desires a sudden display of divine favor in which God overwhelms them with blessings.

5.anchor: will shout for joy when they reap the harvest.
5.note: sn O. Borowski says regarding this passage: “The dependence on rain for watering plants, the uncertainty of the quantity and timing of the rains, and the possibility of crop failure due to pests and diseases appear to have kept the farmer in a gloomy mood during sowing” (Agriculture in Iron Age Israel, 54). Perhaps the people were experiencing a literal drought, the effects of which cause them to lament their plight as they plant their seed in hopes that the rain would come. However, most take the language as metaphorical. Like a farmer sowing his seed, the covenant community was enduring hardship as they waited for a new outpouring of divine blessing. Yet they are confident that a time of restoration will come and relieve their anxiety, just as the harvest brings relief and joy to the farmer.

6.anchor: will certainly come in with a shout of joy, carrying his sheaves of grain.
6.note: tn The Hebrew noun אֲלֻמָּה (’alummah, “sheaf”) occurs only here and in Gen 37:7 in the OT.sn Verse 6 expands the image of v. 5. See the note on the word “harvest” there.

1.note: tn Heb “for forever [is] his loyal love.”

2.note: tn Or “redeemed.”
tn Heb “hand.”

3.note: tn Heb “from lands.” The word “foreign” is supplied in the translation for clarification.

5.anchor: they fainted from exhaustion.
5.note: tn Heb “and their soul in them fainted.”

7.anchor: He led them on a level road,
7.note: sn A level road. See Jer 31:9.

8.anchor: and for the amazing things he has done for people!
8.note: tn Heb “and [for] his amazing deeds for the sons of man.”

9.anchor: and those who hunger he has filled with food.
9.note: tn Heb “and [the] hungry throat he has filled [with] good.”

10.note: tn Heb “those bound in suffering and iron.” “Suffering and iron” is a hendiadys (like English “good and angry”), where both words contribute to one idea. In this case the first word characterizes the second; the iron (chains) contribute to the prisoners’ pain and suffering.

11.anchor: and rejected the instructions of the sovereign king.
11.note: tn Heb “the counsel of the Most High.”

12.anchor: So he used suffering to humble them;
12.note: tn Heb “and he subdued with suffering their heart.”

14.anchor: He brought them out of the utter darkness,
14.note: tn Heb “darkness and deep darkness.” See the note on the word “darkness” in v. 10.

15.anchor: and for the amazing things he has done for people!
15.note: tn Heb “and [for] his amazing deeds for the sons of man.” See v. 8.

16.anchor: and hacked through the iron bars.
16.note: sn The language of v. 16 recalls Isa 45:2.

17.anchor: They acted like fools in their rebellious ways,
17.note: tn Heb “fools [they were] because of the way of their rebellion.”

18.anchor: They lost their appetite for all food,
18.note: tn Heb “all food their appetite loathed.”

20.anchor: he rescued them from the pits where they were trapped.
20.note: tn Heb “he rescued from their traps.” The Hebrew word שְׁחִית (shekhit, “trap”) occurs only here and in Lam 4:20, where it refers to a trap or pit in which one is captured. Because of the rarity of the term and the absence of an object with the verb “rescued,” some prefer to emend the text of Ps 107:20, reading מִשַׁחַת חַיָּתָם (mishakhat khayyatam, “[he rescued] their lives from the pit”). Note also NIV “from the grave,” which interprets the “pit” as Sheol or the grave.

21.anchor: and for the amazing things he has done for people!
21.note: tn Heb “and [for] his amazing deeds for the sons of man.” See v. 8.

22.anchor: and loudly proclaim what he has done!
22.note: tn Heb “and let them proclaim his works with a ringing cry.”

23.anchor: and carried cargo over the vast waters.
23.note: tn Heb “doers of work on the mighty waters.”

25.anchor: and it stirred up the waves of the sea.
25.note: tn Heb “and it stirred up its [i.e., the sea’s, see v. 23] waves.”

26.anchor: The sailors’ strength
26.note: tn Heb “their being”; traditionally “their soul” (referring to that of the sailors). This is sometimes translated “courage” (cf. NIV, NRSV).

26.anchor: left them
26.note: tn Or “melted.”

26.anchor: because the danger was so great.
26.note: tn Heb “from danger.”

27.anchor: and all their skill proved ineffective.
27.note: tn The Hitpael of בָלַע (vala’) occurs only here in the OT. Traditionally the form is derived from the verbal root בלע (“to swallow”), but HALOT 135 s.v. III בלע understands a homonym here with the meaning “to be confused.”

29.note: tn Heb “their waves.” The antecedent of the third masculine plural pronominal suffix is not readily apparent, unless it refers back to “waters” in v. 23.

30.anchor: and he led them to the harbor
30.note: tn The Hebrew noun occurs only here in the OT.

31.anchor: and for the amazing things he has done for people!
31.note: tn Heb “and [for] his amazing deeds for the sons of man.” See v. 8.

32.anchor: Let them praise him in the place where the leaders preside!
32.note: tn Heb “in the seat of the elders.”

33.note: tn The verbal form appears to be a preterite, which is most naturally taken as narrational. (The use of prefixed forms with vav [ו] consecutive in vv. 36-37 favor this.) The psalmist may return to the theme of God’s intervention for the exiles (see vv. 4-22, especially vv. 4-9). However, many regard vv. 33-41 as a hymnic description which generalizes about God’s activities among men. In this case it would be preferable to use the English present tense throughout (cf. NEB, NRSV).

34.anchor: and a fruitful land into a barren place,

35.note: tn The words “As for his people” are not included in the Hebrew text, but have been supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity. The psalmist contrasts God’s judgment on his enemies with his blessing of his people. See the note on the word “enemies” in v. 39 for further discussion.
tn The verbal form appears to be a preterite, which is most naturally taken as narrational. See the note on the word “turned” in v. 33.

37.anchor: which yielded a harvest of fruit.

38.anchor: He would not allow their cattle to decrease in number.
38.note: tn The verbal form in this line appears to be an imperfect, which may be taken as customary (drawing attention to typical action in a past time frame) or as generalizing (in which case one should use the English present tense, understanding a move from narrative to present reality).

39.note: tn Heb “from the oppression of calamity.”

40.note: tn The active participle is understood as past durative here, drawing attention to typical action in a past time frame. However, it could be taken as generalizing (in which case one should translate using the English present tense), in which case the psalmist moves from narrative to present reality. Perhaps the participial form appears because the statement is lifted from Job 12:21.

42.note: tn Heb “all evil,” which stands metonymically for those who do evil.

1.anchor: when the family of Jacob left a foreign nation behind,
1.note: tn Heb “the house of Jacob from a nation speaking a foreign language.” The Hebrew verb לָעַז (la’at, “to speak a foreign language”) occurs only here in the OT.

3.note: tn Heb “the Jordan” (also in v. 5). The word “River” is not in the Hebrew text, but has been supplied in the translation for clarity.
sn The psalmist recalls the crossing of the Jordan River (Josh 3:13, 16).

4.note: sn The mountains skipped like rams, the hills like lambs. This may recall the theophany at Sinai when the mountain shook before God’s presence (Exod 19:18).

8.anchor: a hard rock into springs of water!
8.note: sn In v. 8 the psalmist recalls the event(s) recorded in Exod 17:6 and/or Num 20:11 (see also Deut 8:15 and Ps 78:15-16, 20).

1.anchor: or concern myself with things that are beyond me.
1.note: tn Heb “I do not walk in great things, and in things too marvelous for me.”

2.anchor: I am content like the young child I carry.
2.note: tn Heb “like the weaned [one] upon me, my soul.”

1.note: tn Heb “stand.”

3.note: tn The pronominal suffix is second masculine singular, suggesting that the servants addressed in vv. 1-2 are responding to the psalmist.
tn Heb “may the Lord bless you from Zion, the maker of heaven and earth.”

1.note: tn Heb “all his affliction.” This may refer to David’s strenuous and tireless efforts to make provision for the building of the temple (see 1 Chr 22:14). Some prefer to revocalize the text as עַנַוָתוֹ (’anavato, “his humility”).

2.anchor: and swore an oath to the powerful ruler of Jacob.
2.note: tn Heb “the powerful [one] of Jacob.”

3.note: tn Heb “go up upon the bed of my couch.”

5.anchor: a fine dwelling place
5.note: tn The plural form of the noun may indicate degree or quality; David envisions a special dwelling place (see Pss 43:3; 46:4; 84:1).

5.anchor: for the powerful ruler of Jacob.”
5.note: tn Heb “the powerful [one] of Jacob.”

6.anchor: we found it in the territory of Jaar.
6.note: tn Heb “fields of the forest.” The Hebrew term יָעַר (ya’ad, “forest”) is apparently a shortened alternative name for קִרְיַת יְעָרִים (qiryat yÿ’arim, “Kiriath-jearim”), the place where the ark was kept after it was released by the Philistines and from which David and his men retrieved it (see 1 Chr 13:6).

7.note: tn Or “bow down.”

9.anchor: May your priests be clothed with integrity!
9.note: tn Or “righteousness.”

10.anchor: do not reject your chosen king!
10.note: tn Heb “do not turn away the face of your anointed one.”

11.anchor: He said,
11.note: tn The words “he said” are supplied in the translation to clarify that what follows are the Lord’s words.

11.anchor: “I will place one of your descendants
11.note: tn Heb “the fruit of your body.”

13.anchor: he decided to make it his home.
13.note: tn Heb “he desired it for his dwelling place.”

14.anchor: I will live here, for I have chosen it.
14.note: tn Heb “for I desired it.”

15.anchor: I will give her poor all the food they need.
15.note: tn Heb “her poor I will satisfy [with] food.”

16.anchor: and her godly people will shout exuberantly.
16.note: tn Heb “[with] shouting they will shout.” The infinitive absolute is used to emphasize the verb.

17.anchor: I have determined that my chosen king’s dynasty will continue.
17.note: tn Heb “I have arranged a lamp for my anointed one.” Here the “lamp” is a metaphor for the Davidic dynasty (see 1 Kgs 11:36).

18.note: tn Heb “his enemies I will clothe [with] shame.”

1.note: tn Heb “for forever [is] his loyal love.”

2.anchor: or relate all his praiseworthy deeds?
2.note: tn Heb “[or] cause to be heard all his praise.”

5.anchor: and boast along with the people who belong to you.
5.note: tn Heb “with your inheritance.”

6.note: tn Heb “with.”
tn Heb “fathers” (also in v. 7).

7.anchor: and they rebelled at the sea, by the Red Sea.
7.note: tn Heb “Reed Sea” (also in vv. 9, 22). “Reed Sea” (or “Sea of Reeds”) is a more accurate rendering of the Hebrew expression יָם סוּף (yam suf), traditionally translated “Red Sea.” See the note on the term “Red Sea” in Exod 13:18.sn They rebelled. The psalmist recalls the people’s complaint recorded in Exod 14:12.

8.anchor: Yet he delivered them for the sake of his reputation,
8.note: tn Heb “his name,” which here stands metonymically for God’s reputation.

9.note: tn Or “rebuked.”

10.note: tn Or “redeemed.”
tn Heb “hand.”

11.anchor: not even one of them survived.
11.note: tn Heb “remained.”

12.anchor: They believed his promises;
12.note: tn Heb “his words.”

13.anchor: they did not wait for his instructions.
13.note: tn Heb “his counsel.”

14.note: tn Heb “they tested God.”

15.anchor: then struck them with a disease.
15.note: tn Heb “and he sent leanness into their being.”sn Disease. See Num 11:33-34, where this plague is described.

16.anchor: ’s holy priest.
16.note: tn Heb “the holy one of the Lord.”

17.anchor: it engulfed
17.note: tn Or “covered.”

17.anchor: the group led by Abiram.
17.note: tn Or “the assembly of Abiram.”

18.note: sn Verses 16-18 describe the events of Num 16:1-40.

20.note: tn Heb “their glory.” According to an ancient Hebrew scribal tradition, the text originally read “his glory” or “my glory.” In Jer 2:11 the Lord states that his people (Israel) exchanged “their glory” (a reference to the Lord) for worthless idols.

21.anchor: They rejected
21.note: tn Heb “forgot.”

22.note: tn Or “awe-inspiring.”

23.anchor: and turned back his destructive anger.
23.note: tn Heb “to turn back his anger from destroying.”sn Verses 19-23 describe the events of Exod 32:1-35.

24.anchor: they did not believe his promise.
24.note: tn Heb “his word.”

25.note: tn Heb “did not listen to the voice of.”

26.anchor: that he would make them die
26.note: tn Heb “to cause them to fall.”

27.anchor: and scatter them among foreign lands.
27.note: tn Heb “among the lands.” The word “foreign” is supplied in the translation for clarification.

28.anchor: and ate sacrifices offered to the dead.
28.note: tn Here “the dead” may refer to deceased ancestors (see Deut 26:14). Another option is to understand the term as a derogatory reference to the various deities which the Israelites worshiped at Peor along with Baal (see Num 25:2 and L. C. Allen, Psalms 101-150 [WBC], 49).

29.note: tn Heb “They made angry [him].” The pronominal suffix is omitted here, but does appear in a few medieval Hebrew mss. Perhaps it was accidentally left off, an original וַיַּכְעִיסוּהוּ (vayyakh’isuhu) being misread as וַיַּכְעִיסוּ (vayyakh’isu). In the translation the referent of the pronominal suffix (the Lord) has been specified for clarity to avoid confusion with Baal of Peor (mentioned in the previous verse).

30.anchor: Phinehas took a stand and intervened,
30.note: sn The intervention of Phinehas is recounted in Num 25:7-8.

31.note: tn Heb “and it was reckoned to him for righteousness, to a generation and a generation forever.” The verb חָשַׁב (khashav, “to reckon”) is collocated with צְדָקָה (tsÿdaqah, “righteousness”) only in Ps 106:31 and Gen 15:6, where God rewards Abram’s faith with a land grant.sn Brought him a reward. See Num 25:12-13.

32.note: tn Heb “there was harm to Moses.”

33.note: tn The Hebrew text adds “with his lips,” but this has not been included in the translation for stylistic reasons.sn Verses 32-33 allude to the events of Num 20:1-13.

34.anchor: They did not destroy the nations,
34.note: tn That is, the nations of Canaan.

36.anchor: which became a snare to them.
36.note: sn Became a snare. See Exod 23:33; Judg 2:3.

37.anchor: They sacrificed their sons and daughters to demons.
37.note: tn The Hebrew term שֵׁדִים (shedim, “demons”) occurs only here and in Deut 32:17. Some type of lesser deity is probably in view.

38.anchor: The land was polluted by bloodshed.
38.note: sn Num 35:33-34 explains that bloodshed defiles a land.

39.note: tn Heb “and they committed adultery in their actions.” This means that they were unfaithful to the Lord (see Ps 73:27).

40.anchor: and despised the people who belong to him.

41.note: tn Heb “gave them into the hand of.”

42.anchor: they were subject to their authority.
42.note: tn Heb “they were subdued under their hand.”

43.note: tn Heb “they sank down.” The Hebrew verb מָכַךְ (makhakh, “to lower; to sink”) occurs only here in the Qal.

45.note: tn The Niphal of נָחַם (nakham) refers here to God relenting from a punishment already underway.

47.anchor: and boast about your praiseworthy deeds.
47.note: tn Heb “to boast in your praise.”

48.anchor: Let all the people say, “We agree!

48.note: sn The final verse (v. 48) is a conclusion to this fourth “book” (or major editorial division) of the Psalter. Similar statements appear at or near the end of each of the first, second and third “books” of the Psalter (see Pss 41:13; 72:18-19; 89:52, respectively).

5.anchor: his mighty acts and the judgments he decreed,
5.note: tn Heb “and the judgments of his mouth.”

6.note: tn Heb “sons.”
tn Heb “his”; the referent (God) has been specified in the translation for clarity.

7.anchor: he carries out judgment throughout the earth.
7.note: tn Heb “in all the earth [are] his judgments.”

8.note: tn Heb “[the] word he commanded.” The text refers here to God’s unconditional covenantal promise to Abraham and the patriarchs, as vv. 10-12 make clear.

9.note: tn Heb “which.”

10.anchor: to Israel as a lasting promise,
10.note: tn Or “eternal covenant.”

13.anchor: and from one kingdom to another.
13.note: tn Heb “and from a kingdom to another nation.”

15.anchor: saying,
15.note: tn The word “saying” is supplied in the translation for clarification and for stylistic reasons.

15.anchor: “Don’t touch my chosen
15.note: tn Heb “anointed.”

16.anchor: he cut off all the food supply.
16.note: tn Heb “and every staff of food he broke.” The psalmist refers to the famine that occurred in Joseph’s time (see v. 17 and Gen 41:53-57).

17.anchor: He sent a man ahead of them
17.note: tn After the reference to the famine in v. 16, v. 17 flashes back to events that preceded the famine (see Gen 37).

18.anchor: his neck was placed in an iron collar,
18.note: tn Heb “his neck came [into] iron.” The Hebrew term נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh) with the suffix could mean simply “he” or “his life.” But the nuance “neck” makes good sense here (note the reference to his “feet” in the preceding line). See L. C. Allen, Psalms 101-150 (WBC), 38.

19.note: tn This line may refer to Joseph’s prediction of the famine in response to Pharaoh’s dream. Joseph emphasized to Pharaoh that the interpretation of the dream came from God (see Gen 41:16, 25, 28, 32, 39).
tn Heb “refined him.”

20.note: tn Heb “[the] king sent and set him free.”

21.anchor: He put him in charge of his palace,
21.note: tn Heb “he made him master of his house.”

22.note: tn Heb “and his elders he taught wisdom.”

23.anchor: Jacob lived for a time
23.note: tn Heb “lived as a resident alien.”

24.anchor: and made them
24.note: tn Heb “him,” referring to “his people.”

24.anchor: more numerous than their
24.note: tn Heb “his,” referring to “his people.”

25.note: tn Or “to deal deceptively.” The Hitpael of נָכַל (nakhal) occurs only here and in Gen 37:18, where it is used of Joseph’s brothers “plotting” to kill him.

27.anchor: They executed his miraculous signs among them,
27.note: tn Apparently the pronoun refers to “his servants” (i.e., the Israelites, see v. 25).

28.anchor: they did not disobey his orders.
28.note: tn Heb “they did not rebel against his words.” Apparently this refers to Moses and Aaron, who obediently carried out God’s orders.

31.note: tn Heb “he spoke and flies came.”

32.anchor: there was lightning in their land.
32.note: tn Heb “fire of flames [was] in their land.”

34.note: tn Heb “he spoke and locusts came.”

35.anchor: and devoured the crops of their fields.
35.note: tn Heb “the fruit of their ground.”

36.anchor: the firstfruits of their reproductive power.
36.note: tn Heb “the beginning of all their strength,” that is, reproductive power (see Ps 78:51).sn Verses 28-36 recall the plagues in a different order than the one presented in Exodus: v. 28 (plague 9), v. 29 (plague 1), v. 30 (plague 2), v. 31a (plague 4), v. 31b (plague 3), vv. 32-33 (plague 7), vv. 34-35 (plague 8), v. 36 (plague 10). No reference is made in Ps 105 to plagues 5 and 6.

37.note: tn Heb “them”; the referent (the Lord’s people) has been supplied in the translation for clarity.
tn The word “enriched” is supplied in the translation for clarification and for stylistic reasons.

38.anchor: for they were afraid of them.
38.note: tn Heb “for fear of them had fallen upon them.”

39.anchor: He spread out a cloud for a cover,

40.anchor: he satisfied them with food from the sky.
40.note: tn Or “bread of heaven.” The reference is to manna (see Exod 16:4, 13-15).

43.anchor: his chosen ones shouted with joy.
43.note: tn Heb “and he led his people out with joy, with a ringing cry, his chosen ones.”

44.anchor: and they took possession of what other peoples had produced,
44.note: tn Heb “and the [product of the] work of peoples they possessed.”

4.anchor: he cut the ropes of the wicked.”
4.note: tn The background of the metaphor is not entirely clear. Perhaps the “ropes” are those used to harness the ox for plowing (see Job 39:10). Verse 3 pictures the wicked plowing God’s people as if they were a field. But when God “cut the ropes” of their ox, as it were, they could no longer plow. The point of the metaphor seems to be that God took away the enemies’ ability to oppress his people. See L. C. Allen, Psalms 101-150 (WBC), 187.

6.anchor: which withers before one can even pull it up,
6.note: tn The Hebrew verb שָׁלַף (shalaf) normally means “to draw [a sword]” or “to pull.” BDB 1025 s.v. suggests the meaning “to shoot up” here, but it is more likely that the verb here means “to pluck; to pull up,” a nuance attested for this word in later Hebrew and Aramaic (see Jastrow 1587 s.v. שָׁלַף).

8.anchor: Those who pass by will not say,
8.note: tn The perfect verbal form is used for rhetorical effect; it describes an anticipated development as if it were already reality.

1.note: tn Heb “depths,” that is, deep waters (see Ps 69:2, 14; Isa 51:10), a metaphor for the life-threatening danger faced by the psalmist.

2.note: tn Heb “may your ears be attentive to the voice of.”

3.anchor: O Lord, who could stand before you?
3.note: tn The words “before you” are supplied in the translation for clarification. The psalmist must be referring to standing before God’s judgment seat. The rhetorical question expects the answer, “No one.”

4.note: tn Or “consequently you are.”
tn Heb “feared.”

5.anchor: I wait for his assuring word.
5.note: tn Heb “his word.”

6.anchor: yes, more than watchmen do for the morning.
6.note: tn Heb “more than watchmen for the morning, watchmen for the morning.” The words “yes, more” are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.

7.anchor: and is more than willing to deliver.
7.note: tn Heb “and abundantly with him [is] redemption.”

8.anchor: from all the consequences of their sins.
8.note: tn The Hebrew noun עָוֹן (’avon) can refer to sin, the guilt sin produces, or the consequences of sin. Only here is the noun collocated with the verb פָּדָה (padah, “to redeem; to deliver”). The psalmist may refer to forgiveness per se (v. 4), but the emphasis in this context is likely on deliverance from the national consequences of sin. See L. C. Allen, Psalms 101-150 (WBC), 192.

2.note: tn Heb “stand.”

3.anchor: Sing praises to his name, for it is pleasant!
3.note: tn Heb “for [it is] pleasant.” The translation assumes that it is the Lord’s “name” that is pleasant. Another option is to understand the referent of “it” as the act of praising (see Ps 147:1).

4.anchor: Israel to be his special possession.
4.note: sn His special possession. The language echoes Exod 19:5; Deut 7:6; 14:2; 26:18. See also Mal 3:17.

5.anchor: Yes,
5.note: tn Or “for.”

9.note: tn Or “signs” (see Ps 65:8).
tn Or “portents”; “omens” (see Ps 71:7). The Egyptian plagues are alluded to here.

13.note: tn Heb “O Lord, your remembrance [is] for a generation and a generation.” See Ps 102:12.

14.anchor: and has compassion on his servants.
14.note: sn Verse 14 echoes Deut 32:36, where Moses affirms that God mercifully relents from fully judging his wayward people.

15.anchor: they are man-made.
15.note: tn Heb “the work of the hands of man.”

17.anchor: Indeed, they cannot breathe.
17.note: tn Heb “indeed, there is not breath in their mouth.” For the collocation אַף אֵין (’af ’en, “indeed, there is not”) see Isa 41:26. Another option is to take אַף as “nose” (see Ps 115:6), in which case one might translate, “a nose, [but] they have no breath in their mouths.”

18.anchor: Those who make them will end up
18.note: tn Heb “will be.” Another option is to take the prefixed verbal form as a prayer, “may those who make them end up like them.” sn Because the idols are lifeless, they cannot help their worshipers in times of crisis. Consequently the worshipers end up as dead as the gods in which they trust.

19.note: tn Heb “house” (here and in the next two lines).

20.note: tn Heb “fearers.”

21.anchor: he who dwells in Jerusalem.
21.note: map For location see Map5-B1; Map6-F3; Map7-E2; Map8-F2; Map10-B3; JP1-F4; JP2-F4; JP3-F4; JP4-F4.

1.note: tn Or “is forever.”

13.note: tn Or “cut.”

tn Heb “Reed Sea” (also in v. 15). “Reed Sea” (or “Sea of Reeds”) is a more accurate rendering of the Hebrew expression יָם סוּף (yam suf), traditionally translated “Red Sea.” See the note on the term “Red Sea” in Exod 13:18.
tn Heb “into pieces.”

15.anchor: and tossed
15.note: tn Or “shook off.”

23.anchor: to the one who remembered us when we were down,
23.note: tn Heb “who, in our low condition, remembered us.”

25.anchor: to the one who gives food to all living things,
25.note: tn Heb “to all flesh,” which can refer to all people (see Pss 65:2; 145:21) or more broadly to mankind and animals. Elsewhere the psalms view God as the provider for all living things (see Pss 104:27-28; 145:15).

1.note: tn Heb “there we sit down, also we weep.”

3.anchor: “Sing for us a song about Zion!”
3.note: tn Heb “from a song of Zion.” Most modern translations read, “one of the songs of Zion,” taking the preposition מִן (min, “from”) as partitive and “song” as collective. The present translation assumes the mem (ם) is enclitic, being misunderstood later as the prefixed preposition.

5.anchor: may my right hand be crippled!
5.note: tn Heb “may my right hand forget.” In this case one must supply an object, such as “how to move.” The elliptical nature of the text has prompted emendations (see L. C. Allen, Psalms 101-150 [WBC], 236). The translation assumes an emendation to תִּכְשַׁח (tikhshakh), from an otherwise unattested root כשׁח, meaning “to be crippled; to be lame.” See HALOT 502 s.v. כשׁח, which cites Arabic cognate evidence in support of the proposal. The corruption of the MT can be explained as an error of transposition facilitated by the use of שָׁכַח (shakhakh, “forget”) just before this.

6.anchor: over whatever gives me the most joy.
6.note: tn Heb “if I do not lift up Jerusalem over the top of my joy.”

7.anchor: They said, “Tear it down, tear it down,
7.note: tn Heb “lay [it] bare, lay [it] bare.”

8.anchor: for what you dished out to us!
8.note: tn Heb “O the happiness of the one who repays you your wage which you paid to us.”

9.anchor: and smashes them on a rock!
9.note: sn For other references to the wholesale slaughter of babies in the context of ancient Near Eastern warfare, see 2 Kgs 8:12; Isa 13:16; Hos 13:16; Nah 3:10.

1.note: tn The referent of the Hebrew term אֱלֹהִים (’elohim) is unclear. It refers either to the angelic assembly (see Gen 3:5; Ps 8:5) or to the pagan gods (see Pss 82:1, 6; 86:8; 97:7), in which case the psalmist’s praise takes on a polemical tone.

2.anchor: for you have exalted your promise above the entire sky.
2.note: tc The MT reads, “for you have made great over all your name your word.” If retained, this must mean that God's mighty intervention, in fulfillment of his word of promise, surpassed anything he had done prior to this. However, the statement is odd and several emendations have been proposed. Some read, “for you have exalted over everything your name and your word,” while others suggest, “for you have exalted over all the heavens your name and your word.” The translation assumes an emendation of “your name” to “your heavens” (a construction that appears in Pss 8:3 and 144:5). The point is that God has been faithful to his promise and the reliability of that promise is apparent to all. For a fuller discussion of these options, see L. C. Allen, Psalms 101-150 (WBC), 244.

3.anchor: You made me bold and energized me.
3.note: tn Heb “you made me bold in my soul [with] strength.”

4.anchor: when they hear the words you speak.
4.note: tn Heb “the words of your mouth.”

5.anchor: ’s splendor is magnificent.
5.note: tn Heb “great.”

7.note: tn Heb “against the anger of my enemies you extend your hand.”

8.anchor: Do not abandon those whom you have made!
8.note: tn Heb “the works of your hands.” Many medieval Hebrew mss read the singular, “work of your hands.”

1.note: tn Heb “a man of violent acts.” The Hebrew uses the singular in a representative or collective sense (note the plural verbs in v. 2).

2.anchor: All day long they stir up conflict.
2.note: tc Heb “they attack [for] war.” Some revocalize the verb (which is a Qal imperfect from גּוּר, gur, “to attack”) as יְגָרוּ (yÿgaru), a Piel imperfect from גָרָה (garah, “stir up strife”). This is followed in the present translation.

3.note: tn The Hebrew term is used only here in the OT.
tn Heb “under.”

4.anchor: who plan to knock me over.
4.note: tn Heb “to push down my steps.”

5.anchor: evil men
5.note: tn Heb “and ropes,” but many prefer to revocalize the noun as a participle (חֹבְלִים, khovÿlim) from the verb חָבַל (khaval, “act corruptly”).

7.note: tn Heb “cover.”

8.note: tn Heb “his.” The singular is used in a representative sense (see v. 1).

8.anchor: plan to succeed when they attack!
8.note: tn Heb “his plot do not promote, they rise up.” The translation understands the final verb as being an unmarked temporal clause. Another option is to revocalize the verb as a Hiphil and take the verb with the next verse, “those who surround me lift up [their] head,” which could refer to their proud attitude as they anticipate victory (see Ps 27:6).

9.note: tn Heb “harm of their lips.” The genitive here indicates the source or agent of the harm.

10.anchor: From bottomless pits they will not escape.
10.note: tn Heb “into bottomless pits, they will not arise.” The translation assumes that the preposition -בְּ (bet) has the nuance “from” here. Another option is to connect the line with what precedes, take the final clause as an asyndetic relative clause, and translate, “into bottomless pits [from which] they cannot arise.” The Hebrew noun מַהֲמֹרָה (mahamorah, “bottomless pit”) occurs only here in the OT.

11.anchor: calamity will hunt down a violent man and strike him down.
11.note: tn Heb “for blows.” The Hebrew noun מַדְחֵפֹה (madkhefoh, “blow”) occurs only here in the OT.

12.anchor: and vindicates the poor.
12.note: tn Heb “and the just cause of the poor.”

1.note: tn The statement is understood as generalizing – the psalmist describes what God typically does.

3.anchor: you are aware of everything I do.
3.note: tn Heb “all my ways.”

4.note: tn Heb “look, O Lord, you know all of it.”

6.anchor: it is so far beyond me, I am unable to fathom it.
6.note: tn Heb “too amazing [is this] knowledge for me, it is elevated, I cannot attain to it.”

7.anchor: Where can I flee to escape your presence?
7.note: tn Heb “Where can I go from your spirit, and where from your face can I flee?” God’s “spirit” may refer here (1) to his presence (note the parallel term, “your face,” and see Ps 104:29-30, where God’s “face” is his presence and his “spirit” is the life-giving breath he imparts) or (2) to his personal Spirit (see Ps 51:10).

8.anchor: If I were to sprawl out in Sheol, there you would be.
8.note: tn Heb “look, you.”

9.anchor: and settle down on the other side
9.note: tn Heb “at the end.”

11.anchor: and the light will turn to night all around me,”
11.note: tn Heb “and night, light, around me.”

12.anchor: darkness and light are the same to you.
12.note: tn Heb “like darkness, like light.”

13.note: tn The Hebrew verb סָכַךְ (sakhakh, “to weave together”) is an alternate form of שָׂכַךְ (sakhakh, “to weave”) used in Job 10:11.

14.note: tc Heb “and my being knows very much.” Better parallelism is achieved (see v. 15a) if one emends יֹדַעַת (yoda’at), a Qal active participle, feminine singular form, to יָדַעְתָּ (yada’ta), a Qal perfect second masculine singular perfect. See L. C. Allen, Psalms 101-150 (WBC), 252.

15.anchor: and sewed together in the depths of the earth.
15.note: sn The phrase depths of the earth may be metaphorical (euphemistic) or it may reflect a prescientific belief about the origins of the embryo deep beneath the earth’s surface (see H. W. Wolff, Anthropology of the Old Testament, 96-97). Job 1:21 also closely associates the mother’s womb with the earth.

16.anchor: before one of them came into existence.
16.note: tn Heb “and on your scroll all of them were written, [the] days [which] were formed, and [there was] not one among them.” This “scroll” may be the “scroll of life” mentioned in Ps 69:28 (see the note on the word “living” there).

17.anchor: How vast is their sum total!
17.note: tn Heb “how vast are their heads.” Here the Hebrew word “head” is used of the “sum total” of God’s knowledge of the psalmist.

18.anchor: I would still have to contend with you.
18.note: tc Heb “I awake and I [am] still with you.” A reference to the psalmist awaking from sleep makes little, if any, sense contextually. For this reason some propose an emendation to הֲקִצּוֹתִי (haqitsoti), a Hiphil perfect form from an otherwise unattested verb קָצַץ (qatsats) understood as a denominative of קֵץ (qets, “end”). See L. C. Allen, Psalms 101-150 (WBC), 252-53.

19.anchor: Get away from me, you violent men!
19.note: tn Heb “men of bloodshed.”

20.note: tc Heb “lifted up for emptiness, your cities.” The Hebrew text as it stands makes no sense. The form נָשֻׂא (nasu’; a Qal passive participle) should be emended to נָשְׂאוּ (nosÿu; a Qal perfect, third common plural, “[they] lift up”). Many emend עָרֶיךָ (’arekha, “your cities”) to עָלֶיךָ (’alekha, “against you”), but it is preferable to understand the noun as an Aramaism and translate “your enemies” (see Dan 4:16 and L. C. Allen, Psalms 101-150 [WBC], 253).

21.anchor: and despise those who oppose you?
21.note: tc Heb “who raise themselves up against you.” The form וּבִתְקוֹמְמֶיךָ (uvitqomÿmekha) should be emended to וּבְמִתְקוֹמְמֶיךָ (uvÿmitqomÿmekha), a Hitpolel participle (the prefixed mem [מ] of the participle is accidentally omitted in the MT, though a few medieval Hebrew mss have it).

22.note: tn Heb “[with] completeness of hatred I hate them.”

23.anchor: Test me, and know my concerns!
23.note: tn The Hebrew noun שַׂרְעַפַּי (sar’apay, “concerns”) is used of “worries” in Ps 94:19.

24.anchor: and lead me in the reliable ancient path!
24.note: tn Heb “in the path of antiquity.” This probably refers to the moral path prescribed by the Lord at the beginning of Israel’s history. See Jer 6:16; 18:15, as well as L. C. Allen, Psalms 101-150 (WBC), 253.

2.anchor: my uplifted hands like the evening offering!
2.note: tn Heb “may my prayer be established [like] incense before you, the uplifting of my hands [like] an evening offering.”

3.note: tn Heb “door.” The Hebrew word occurs only here in the OT.
sn My mouth…my lips. The psalmist asks God to protect him from speaking inappropriately or sinfully.

4.anchor: I will not eat their delicacies.
4.note: sn Their delicacies. This probably refers to the enjoyment that a sinful lifestyle appears to offer.

5.anchor: Indeed, my prayer is a witness against their evil deeds.
5.note: tc Heb “for still, and my prayer [is] against their evil deeds.” The syntax of the Hebrew text is difficult; the sequence -כִּי־עוֹד וּ (kiy-’od u-, “for still and”) occurs only here. The translation assumes an emendation to כִּי עֵד תְפלָּתִי (“indeed a witness [is] my prayer”). The psalmist’s lament about the evil actions of sinful men (see v. 4) testifies against the wicked in the divine court.

6.note: tn It is unclear how this statement relates to the preceding sentence. Perhaps the judges are the referent of the pronominal subject (“they”) of the verb “will listen,” and “my words” are the referent of the pronominal subject (“they”) of the phrase “are pleasant.” The psalmist may be affirming here his confidence that he will be vindicated when he presents his case before the judges, while the wicked will be punished.

7.anchor: As when one plows and breaks up the soil,
7.note: tn Heb “like splitting and breaking open in the earth.” The meaning of the statement and the point of the comparison are not entirely clear. Perhaps the psalmist is suggesting that he and other godly individuals are as good as dead; their bones are scattered about like dirt that is dug up and tossed aside.

8.anchor: Do not expose me to danger!
8.note: tn Heb “do not lay bare my life.” Only here is the Piel form of the verb collocated with the term נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh, “life”). In Isa 53:12 the Lord’s servant “lays bare (the Hiphil form of the verb is used) his life to death.”

9.anchor: and the traps the evildoers have set.
9.note: tn Heb “and the traps of the doers of evil.”

10.note: tn Heb “at the same [that] I, until I pass by.” Another option is to take יַחַד (yakhad) with the preceding line, “let the wicked fall together into their own nets.”

1.note: sn This statement refers to the extended family structure of ancient Israel, where brothers would often live in proximity to one another (Deut 25:5), giving the family greater social prominence and security. However, in its later application in the Israelite cult it probably envisions unity within the covenant community. See L. C. Allen, Psalms 101-150 (WBC), 212-15.

2.anchor: and then flows down his garments.
2.note: tn Heb “which goes down in accordance with his measured things.” The Hebrew phrase מִדּוֹתָיו (middotayv, “his measured things”) refers here to the robes worn by Aaron. HALOT 546 s.v. *מַד derives the form from מַד (midah, “robe”) rather than מִדָּה (middah, “measured thing”). Ugaritic md means “robe” and is pluralized mdt.

3.anchor: a blessing will be available – eternal life.
3.note: tn Heb “there the Lord has commanded the blessing, life forever.”

1.anchor: I will praise your name continually!
1.note: tn Or, hyperbolically, “forever.”

2.anchor: I will praise your name continually!
2.note: tn Or, hyperbolically, “forever.”

3.anchor: No one can fathom his greatness!
3.note: tn Heb “and concerning his greatness there is no searching.”

4.anchor: and tell about your mighty acts!
4.note: tn The prefixed verbal forms in v. 4 are understood as imperfects, indicating how the psalmist expects his audience to respond to his praise. Another option is to take the forms as jussives, indicating the psalmist’s wish, “may one generation praise…and tell about.”

5.anchor: and your amazing deeds!
5.note: tn Heb “the splendor of the glory of your majesty, and the matters of your amazing deeds I will ponder.”

6.note: tn The prefixed verbal form is understood as an imperfect, indicating how the psalmist expects his audience to respond to his praise. Another option is to take the forms as a jussive, indicating the psalmist’s wish, “may they proclaim.”

7.note: tn The prefixed verbal forms in v. 7 are understood as imperfects, indicating how the psalmist expects his audience to respond to his praise. Another option is to take the forms as jussives, indicating the psalmist’s wish, “may they talk…and sing.”

8.note: tn Heb “slow to anger” (see Pss 86:15; 103:8).
tn Heb “and great of loyal love” (see Pss 86:15; 103:8).

9.anchor: and has compassion on all he has made.
9.note: tn Heb “and his compassion is over all his works.”

12.anchor: so that mankind
12.note: tn Heb “the sons of man.”

13.anchor: Your kingdom is an eternal kingdom,
13.note: tn Heb “a kingdom of all ages.”

14.anchor: and lifts up all who are bent over.
14.note: tn Perhaps “discouraged” (see Ps 57:6).

15.anchor: and you provide them with food on a regular basis.
15.note: tn Heb “and you give to them their food in its season” (see Ps 104:27).

16.anchor: and fill every living thing with the food they desire.
16.note: tn Heb “[with what they] desire.”

17.anchor: and exhibits love in all he does.
17.note: tn Heb “and [is] loving in all his deeds.”

18.anchor: all who cry out to him sincerely.
18.note: tn Heb “in truth.”

19.note: tn In this context “desire” refers to the followers’ desire to be delivered from wicked enemies.
tn Heb “the desire of those who fear him, he does.”

21.anchor: Let all who live
21.note: tn Heb “all flesh.”

1.note: tn Or “for.”

2.note: map For location see Map5-B1; Map6-F3; Map7-E2; Map8-F2; Map10-B3; JP1-F4; JP2-F4; JP3-F4; JP4-F4.

3.note: tn Heb “the one who heals.”

5.anchor: there is no limit to his wisdom.
5.note: tn Heb “to his wisdom there is no counting.”

6.note: tn Heb “brings down.”

7.note: tn Heb “sing to the Lord with thanksgiving.”

8.anchor: and causes grass to grow on the hillsides.
8.note: tn Heb “hills.”

9.anchor: and to the young ravens when they chirp.
9.note: tn Heb “which cry out.”

10.anchor: nor is he impressed by the warrior’s strong legs.
10.note: tn Heb “he does not desire the strength of the horse, he does not take delight in the legs of the man.” Here “the horse” refers to the war horse used by ancient Near Eastern chariot forces, and “the man” refers to the warrior whose muscular legs epitomize his strength.

11.anchor: takes delight in his faithful followers,
11.note: tn Heb “those who fear him.”

13.note: tn Heb “your sons.”

14.note: tn Heb “satisfies you with.”

15.anchor: swiftly his order reaches its destination.
15.note: tn Heb “swiftly his word runs.”

16.anchor: he spreads the frost that is white like ashes.
16.note: tn Heb “the one who gives snow like wool, frost like ashes he scatters.”

17.anchor: Who can withstand the cold wind he sends?
17.note: tn Heb “Before his cold, who can stand?”

18.note: tn Heb “he blows his breath.”

2.anchor: for no one alive is innocent before you.
2.note: tn Heb “for no one living is innocent before you.”

3.note: tn Or “sit.”
sn Dark regions refers to Sheol, which the psalmist views as a dark place located deep in the ground (see Ps 88:6).

4.note: tn Heb “in my midst my heart is shocked.” For a similar use of the Hitpolel of שָׁמֵם (shamem), see Isa 59:16; 63:5.

5.anchor: I reflect on your accomplishments.
5.note: tn Heb “the work of your hands.”

6.anchor: my soul thirsts for you in a parched
6.note: tn Heb “faint” or “weary.” See Ps 63:1.

tc Heb “my soul like a faint land for you.” A verb (perhaps “thirsts”) is implied (see Ps 63:1). The translation assumes an emendation of the preposition -כְּ (kÿ, “like”) to -בְּ (bÿ, “in,” see Ps 63:1; cf. NEB “athirst for thee in a thirsty land”). If the MT is retained, one might translate, “my soul thirsts for you, as a parched land does for water/rain” (cf. NIV, NRSV).

7.note: tn Heb “I will be equal with.”
tn Heb “the pit.” The Hebrew noun בּוֹר (bor, “pit; cistern”) is sometimes used of the grave and/or the realm of the dead. See Ps 28:1.

8.note: tn Heb “for to you I lift up my life.” The Hebrew expression נָאָשׂ נֶפֶשׁ (na’as nefesh, “to lift up [one’s] life”) means “to desire; to long for” (see Deut 24:15; Prov 19:18; Jer 22:27; 44:14; Hos 4:8, as well as H. W. Wolff, Anthropology of the Old Testament, 16).

9.anchor: I run to you for protection.
9.note: tn Heb “to you I cover,” which makes no sense. The translation assumes an emendation to נַסְתִּי (nastiy, “I flee,” a Qal perfect, first singular form from נוּס, nos). Confusion of kaf (כ) and nun (נ) is attested elsewhere (see P. K. McCarter, Textual Criticism [GBS], 48). The collocation of נוּס (“flee”) with אֶל (’el, “to”) is well-attested.

10.note: tn The prefixed verbal form is taken as a jussive. Taking the statement as a prayer fits well with the petitionary tone of vv. 7-10a.
sn A level land (where one can walk free of obstacles) here symbolizes divine blessing and protection. See Pss 26:12 and 27:11 for similar imagery.

11.anchor: Because of your justice, rescue me from trouble!
11.note: tn Heb “by your justice bring out my life from trouble.”

12.anchor: Annihilate
12.note: tn The perfect with vav (ו) consecutive carries on the mood of the preceding imperfect.

12.anchor: all who threaten my life,
12.note: tn Heb “all the enemies of my life.”

2.anchor: Praise him, all his heavenly assembly!
2.note: tn Heb “all his host.”

3.anchor: Praise him, all you shiny stars!
3.note: tn Heb “stars of light.”

4.anchor: and you waters above the sky!
4.note: sn The “water” mentioned here corresponds to the “waters above” mentioned in Gen 1:7. See also Ps 104:3. For a discussion of the picture envisioned by the psalmist, see L. I. J. Stadelmann, The Hebrew Conception of the World, 47.

6.anchor: he issued a decree that will not be revoked.
6.note: tn Heb “and it will not pass away.”

8.anchor: O stormy wind that carries out his orders,
8.note: tn Heb “[that] does his word.”

11.anchor: you princes and all you leaders
11.note: tn Or “judges.”

14.anchor: the Israelites, the people who are close to him.
14.note: tn “[there is] praise for all his loyal followers, to the sons of Israel, the people near him.” Here “praise” stands by metonymy for the victory that prompts it.

1.note: tn Heb “[with] my voice to the Lord I plead for mercy.”

2.note: tn Heb “my trouble before him I declare.”

3.note: tn Heb “you know my path.”

4.anchor: no one is concerned about my life.
4.note: tn Heb “there is no one who seeks for the sake of my life.”

5.anchor: my security
5.note: tn Heb “my portion.” The psalmist compares the Lord to landed property, which was foundational to economic stability in ancient Israel.

6.anchor: for I am in serious trouble!
6.note: tn Heb “for I am very low.”

7.note: tn The Hebrew idiom גָּמַל עַל (gamal ’al) means “to repay,” here in a positive sense.

1.anchor: the one who trains my hands for battle,
1.note: sn The one who trains my hands for battle. The psalmist attributes his skill with weapons to divine enablement (see Ps 18:34). Egyptian reliefs picture gods teaching the king how to shoot a bow. See O. Keel, The Symbolism of the Biblical World, 265.

2.anchor: who makes nations submit to me.
2.note: tn Heb “the one who subdues nations beneath me.”

3.note: tn Heb “and the son of man.” The phrase “son of man” is used here in a collective sense and refers to human beings. For other uses of the phrase in a collective or representative manner, see Num 23:19; Ps 146:3; Isa 51:12.

3.anchor: that you should be concerned about them?
3.note: tn Heb “take account of him.” The two imperfect verbal forms in v. 4 describe God’s characteristic activity.

4.anchor: their days like a shadow that disappears.
4.note: tn Heb “his days [are] like a shadow that passes away,” that is, like a late afternoon shadow made by the descending sun that will soon be swallowed up by complete darkness. See Ps 102:11.

5.anchor: Touch the mountains and make them smolder!
5.note: tn Heb “so they might smolder.” The prefixed verbal form with vav (ו) conjunctive indicates purpose after the preceding imperative.

6.anchor: Shoot your arrows and rout them!
6.note: sn Arrows and lightning bolts are associated in other texts (see Pss 18:14; 77:17-18; Zech 9:14), as well as in ancient Near Eastern art (see R. B. Chisholm, “An Exegetical and Theological study of Psalm 18/2 Samuel 22” [Th.D. diss., Dallas Theological Seminary, 1983], 187).

7.note: tn Heb “from the hand of the sons of foreignness.”

8.note: tn Heb “who [with] their mouth speak falsehood, and their right hand is a right hand of falsehood.” The reference to the “right hand” is probably a metonymy for an oath. When making an oath, one would raise the hand as a solemn gesture. See Exod 6:8; Num 14:30; Deut 32:40. The figure thus represents the making of false oaths (false promises).

10.anchor: and rescued David his servant from a deadly
10.note: tn Heb “harmful.”

11.anchor: and make false promises.
11.note: tn Heb “who [with] their mouth speak falsehood, and their right hand is a right hand of falsehood.” See v. 8 where the same expression occurs.

12.anchor: carved like those in a palace.
12.note: tn Heb “carved [in] the pattern of a palace.”

13.note: tn Heb “they are innumerable.”
tn Heb “in outside places.” Here the term refers to pastures and fields (see Job 5:10; Prov 8:26).

14.anchor: and there will be no terrified cries in our city squares.
14.note: tn Heb “there [will be] no breach, and there [will be] no going out, and there [will be] no crying out in our broad places.”

15.anchor: How blessed are the people who experience these things!
15.note: tn Heb “[O] the happiness of the people who [it is] such to them.”

1.anchor: Praise him in the assembly of the godly!
1.note: tn Heb “his praise in the assembly of the godly ones.”

2.note: tn Heb “sons.”

2.anchor: of Zion delight in their king!
2.note: sn The Lord is the king here, as the parallelism in the previous line (“their creator”) indicates.

4.anchor: he exalts the oppressed by delivering them.
4.note: tn Heb “he honors the oppressed [with] deliverance.”

5.anchor: Let them shout for joy upon their beds!
5.note: tn The significance of the reference to “beds” is unclear. Perhaps the point is that they should rejoice at all times, even when falling asleep or awaking.

6.anchor: while they hold a two-edged sword in their hand,
6.note: tn Heb “[May] praises of God [be] in their throat, and a two-edged sword in their hand.”

7.note: tn Heb “to do.”

8.note: tn Heb “to bind.”

9.anchor: All his loyal followers will be vindicated.
9.note: tn Heb “it is honor for all his godly ones.” The judgment of the oppressive kings will bring vindication and honor to God’s people (see vv. 4-5).

1.anchor: Praise him in the sky, which testifies to his strength!
1.note: tn Heb “the sky of his strength.”

3.anchor: or in human beings, who cannot deliver!
3.note: tn Heb “in a son of man, to whom there is no deliverance.”

4.anchor: on that day their plans die.
4.note: tn Heb “his spirit goes out, it returns to his ground; in that day his plans die.” The singular refers to the representative man mentioned in v. 3b.

6.note: tn Heb “the one who guards faithfulness forever.”

7.note: tn Heb “executes justice for the oppressed.”

8.anchor: lifts up all who are bent over.
8.note: tn Perhaps “discouraged” (see Ps 57:6).

9.note: tn Heb “he makes the way of the wicked twisted.” The “way of the wicked” probably refers to their course of life (see Prov 4:19; Jer 12:1). God makes their path tortuous in the sense that he makes them pay the harmful consequences of their actions.

10.anchor: your God, O Zion, throughout the generations to come!
10.note: tn Heb “for a generation and a generation.”
